(1995b) all yield considerable lower integrated line fluxes ( 8.4-9.0 ), while our new integrated flux value is somewhat lower than the Arecibo measurement of Haynes et al. (1999 13.29 ) and the VLA measurement of Pickering et al. (1999; 13.6 ). CO(1-0) and CO(2-1) line emission were detected from UGC 2936 at SEST and IRAM by Chini et al. (1996), making this one of the rare galaxies classified as an LSB Giant that has been detected in CO (see also Knezek 1993,1998).

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 2936 as having a nucleus, spiral-arm H II regions scattered across the disk, outer clustered H II regions, and only nuclear emission in the other narrowband map.

quotes.quote: Our R band image of UGC 2936 (Figure f:ugc2936 ) 12 reveals the nucleus and H ii regions along spiral arms. These are clearly visible in the map, and are seen scattered throughout the disk out to 68 (16.7 kpc) to the NE and 58 (14.3 kpc) to the SW of the nucleus. A few faint clustered H ii regions are visible beyond what seems to be the rim of the outer spiral arm. The map is remarkably featureless, apart from emission from the nucleus.

gmw_00004612.name: NGC 7589
gmw_00004612.overview: NGC 7589 is a giant low-surface-brightness galaxy characterized by a double structure containing an inner high-surface-brightness spiral galaxy and an outer extended low-surface-brightness disk [arXiv:1003.1312, arXiv:2004.14458]. This extended disk may have been shaped by interactions with its massive, disturbed companion galaxy NGC 7603 [arXiv:2004.14458].

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes NGC 7589 as a prototypical giant low-surface-brightness galaxy, finding a steep central rotation curve, possible baryon dominance, double structure, and a MOND fit that works well.

quotes.quote: We present here a new study of two prototypical GLSB galaxies: Malin 1 and NGC 7589. We re-analysed existing observations and derived new rotation curves, which were used to investigate the distributions of luminous and dark matter in these galaxies. In contrast to previous findings, the rotation curves of both galaxies show a steep rise in the central parts, typical of high surface brightness (HSB) systems.

Mass decompositions with a dark matter halo show that baryons may dominate the dynamics of the inner regions. Indeed, a ``maximum disk'' fit gives stellar mass-to-light ratios in the range of values typically found for HSB galaxies. These results, together with other recent studies, suggest that GLSB galaxies are systems with a double structure: an inner HSB early-type spiral galaxy and an outer extended LSB disk. We also tested the predictions of MOND: the rotation curve of NGC 7589 is reproduced well, whereas Malin 1 represents a challenging test for the theory.

sources.summary: NGC 7589 is modeled with thick, HSB, and LSB disc components; multiple fits depend on inclination and thickness, making model discrimination difficult.

quotes.quote: NGC 7589 The surface density profile is assumed to be decomposed into three parts: a thick disc with an exponential distribution _s ( _ cs =3 \ 10 ^3 \ M_ \ pc ^ 2 , r_ ds =1 \ kpc ), a thin HSB disc _ HSB ( _ cHSB =400 \ M_ \ pc ^ 2 , r_ dHSB =5.5 \ kpc ) and a thin LSB disc ( _ cLSB =10 \ M_ \ pc ^ 2 , r_ dLSB =15 \ kpc ) (Fig. 10). A good fit for the velocity curve is obtained with an inclination of 45 ^ . The latter value sensibly differs from that given by Lelli et al. (2010), with i=58^ . The mean rotation curves are displayed in Fig. 11. Another similar solution is still to take 3 _ LSB =3 _ HSB = _s= _ at constant , while now maintaining the inclination suggested by those authors.

A mixture of intermediary solutions is clearly possible by exploiting both the inclination and \ the thickness. NGC \ 7589 provides a good example indicating that discrimination between different models can be challenging owing to the indeterminacy impacting these parameters when exploiting the observational material.

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 7589 with VLA mapping, attributes possible extra low-velocity flux to F893-29, finds integrated flux agreement, and corrects linewidths for contamination.

quotes.quote: NGC 7589 : NGC 7589 was mapped in \ at the VLA by Pickering et al. (1997). Our new \ profile hints at a possible small amount of additional flux on the low-velocity side of the profile compared with the VLA global spectrum; this is probably caused by confusion with F893-29, which was also detected in \ at the VLA.

Our new integrated line flux (2.96 ) agrees well with that reported by Pickering et al (1997; 2.7 ), hence the flux contamination from this second source appears to be minimal. However our line profile widths appear to be overestimated by 100 \ due to this second source, hence corrections have been applied to the W_ 20 and W_ 50 measurements in Tables 2 4.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7589 as having a massive disturbed companion, NGC 7603, suggesting interaction could explain its extended disc, although gas accretion remains possible because it has both HSB and LSB discs.

quotes.quote: NGC 7589 possesses massive companion NGC 7603 which has disturbed spiral arms. Both extended disc of NGC 7589 and the disturbed appearance of NGC 7603 could be the traces of interaction of these two galaxies. The gas accretion scenario however could not be fully excluded since NGC 7589 has HSB and LSB discs Lelli2010 .

gmw_00004613.name: F893-29
gmw_00004613.overview: F893-29 is a galaxy located southwest of the low surface brightness giant galaxy NGC 7589 [arXiv:astro-ph/0010075]. It has been classified as either an Sb spiral or a dwarf galaxy, and it has been detected in neutral hydrogen [arXiv:astro-ph/0010075].

sources.summary: The paper identifies F893-29 as an Sb or dwarf galaxy southwest of NGC 7589, detected in H I at the VLA with velocity matching extra flux in NGC 7589’s profile.

quotes.quote: Our new \ profile hints at a possible small amount of additional flux on the low-velocity side of the profile compared with the VLA global spectrum; this is probably caused by confusion with F893-29, which was also detected in \ at the VLA. F893-29 is a 17.1 mag Sb? spiral (NED) or dwarf (Pickering et al. 1997), 3 3 SW of the LSB Giant, with a E-W separation of 2 2 . F893-29 has a systemic \ velocity of 8768 \ (Pickering et al. 1997), consistent with the velocity of the ``extra'' flux we observe associated with our global profile of NGC 7589.

gmw_00004614.name: NGC 4751
gmw_00004614.overview: NGC 4751 is an early-type galaxy [arXiv:2404.11260] with a debated morphology, classified as either an elliptical or a lenticular galaxy [arXiv:2404.11260]. It features a fast-rotating stellar component [arXiv:1306.1124], a regularly rotating central molecular gas disc [arXiv:2404.16345], and ionized gas in its cold disks [arXiv:2303.01495].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes NGC 4751 as an early-type galaxy with debated S0 or elliptical morphology, a rotating molecular gas disc, and a massive central black hole measured from gas dynamics.

quotes.quote: NGC 4751 is an ETG located at 12^ h 52^ m 50 79 , 42 39 35 7 (J2000.0). Throughout this paper we adopt a distance D=26.9 Mpc, that was calculated using the galaxy heliocentric velocity corrected for infall of the Local Group into the Virgo Cluster of galaxies and assuming a Hubble constant H_0=72 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 Rusli2013 . At this distance, 1 arcsec corresponds to 130 pc. NGC 4751 has a total absolute B band magnitude of −19.71 and a total absolute V band magnitude of −20.75 Rusli2013 .

There is some disagreement as to whether NGC 4751 is an elliptical or a lenticular galaxy. Images of NGC 4751 suggest that it is comprised of two components, a relatively round central component with a steep radial surface brightness profile and an outer component that is flatter and has a shallower surface brightness profile. This is a defining characteristic of S0 galaxies, and if the central component is interpreted as a bulge and the outer component as a disc, the bulge-to-total luminosity ratio B/T=0.55 0.05 Kormendy2013 . Thus, canonically-speaking, NGC 4751 should be classified as an S0 galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 4751 as a rotating ETG example whose LOSVD wings remain stationary, notes significant cold-disk emission regions, and finds literature kinematics consistent with MUSE measurements.

quotes.quote: The high-velocity wings that we observe in many galaxies do not follow the velocity shifts of the LOSVD peaks in galaxies with rotation: While the main trunk of the LOSVD is centered on different v_ los in different places of a rotating galaxy the wings remain stationary relative to v_ los 0 (e.g. NGC 4751 and NGC 6861 in Fig.  fig:FRellipses ). This makes template mismatch unlikely as the origin of the wings.

For galaxies with spatial regions with significant emission lines -- which in the first place meant ionized gas in the cold disks of NGC 6861 and NGC 4751 -- we attempted to at first fit their spectra with a stellar and an emission component while masking NaD.

NGC 4751 NGC 5516 : for these two galaxies the available WiFes-based kinematics were based on observations with very poor signal Rusli2013b . Therefore both h_ 3 and h_ 4 were essentially not resolved. Nonetheless, within the uncertainties and for v_ rot and , the kinematics match our own measurements.

sources.summary: The paper finds a slightly-more-than-twofold effective gradient in NGC 4751, but treats its kinematics cautiously because dust may bias the excluded quadrant and fits.

quotes.quote: NGC 4751 : As with NGC 1332, we find a gradient within the bulge component, which in superposition with the constant- disc component produces an effective total gradient of slightly more than a factor two. The maximum of the gradient, within < 0.1 kpc , matches our previously published constant- value from R+13.

NGC 4751 : Considering the distribution of the dust in NGC 4751 (see Figure fig:dust4751 ), the dust appears to be somewhat evenly distributed within r_e . However, the distribution of dust is slightly more extended on the south and west side from the center. The quadrant which we had to exclude, q3, is the south western quadrant of the galaxy. The effects of the dust on the kinematics could potentially explain why the ( ^2 + m_ eff )/N of our fits was higher in this galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4751 is a fast-rotating early-type galaxy with high dispersion, a steep central dispersion gradient, and a black hole unusually large for its luminosity.

quotes.quote: Four galaxies exhibit regular rotation: NGC 1374, NGC 4751, NGC 6861 and NGC 7619. In these galaxies, h_3 is anti-correlated with the rotational velocity, matching a widely observed trend (e.g. Bender-94,Krajnovic-08,Krajnovic-11,Fabricius-12 ).
NGC 4751 and NGC 6861 show the fastest rotation and the highest dispersion. A very steep gradient of is seen in NGC 4751: from just below 500 \ in the center down to 250 within the 1.5 arcsec radius.
NGC 4751 deviates the most in the \ relation: the galaxy luminosity is approximately a factor 4 too small for its black hole mass.

sources.summary: The appendix reports an ALMA CO measurement for NGC 4751, where a rotating central molecular disc with Keplerian motions supports SMBH mass estimates under alternative stellar mass-to-light models.

quotes.quote: The SMBH mass of the early-type galaxy NGC 4751 is measured by using ALMA observations of the ^ 12 CO(3–2) line with an angular resolution of 24 pc ( 0.19 arcsec). The observations reveal a regularly rotating central molecular gas disc with clear central Keplerian motions. By forward-modelling the molecular gas kinematics and data cube, infer a SMBH mass M_ BH =3.43^ +0.45 _ 0.44 10^9 M _ assuming a constant stellar M/L , but a SMBH mass M_ BH =2.79_ 0.57 ^ +0.75 10^9 M _ assuming a linearly spatially-varying M/L . We adopt the linearly varying M/L model as it agrees more closely with the SMBH mass derived through stellar kinematics in the same paper.

gmw_00004615.name: J1205 0000
gmw_00004615.overview: J1205 0000 is a red quasar at a redshift of z = 6.72 [arXiv:2101.01199], making it the most distant red quasar known [arXiv:2101.01199]. It exhibits bright far-infrared continuum emission [arXiv:2101.01199] and extended [C II] line emission that may indicate companion galaxies or massive outflows [arXiv:2101.01199].

sources.summary: The paper presents ALMA observations of the z=6.72 red quasar J1205 0000, finding bright FIR emission, extended [C II], and ambiguous merger or outflow signatures.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The continuum peaks at (RA, Dec) _ ICRS = (12 ^ h 05 ^ m 05 ^ s .080, 00 00 28 .04), which is consistent with the optical quasar position.

quotes.quote: We present ALMA [ C 2 ] 158 m line and far-infrared (FIR) continuum emission observations toward HSC J120505.09 000027.9 (J1205 0000) at z = 6.72 with the beam size of 0 .8 0 .5 (or 4.1 kpc 2.6 kpc), the most distant red quasar known to date. Red quasars are modestly reddened by dust, and are thought to be in rapid transition from an obscured starburst to an unobscured normal quasar, driven by powerful active galactic nucleus (AGN) feedback which blows out a cocoon of interstellar medium (ISM). The FIR continuum of J1205 0000 is bright, with an estimated luminosity of L_ FIR 3 10^ 12 L_ .

The [ C 2 ] line emission is extended on scales of 5 kpc, greater than the FIR continuum. The line profiles at the extended regions are complex and broad (FWHM 630-780 km s ^ 1 ). Although it is not practical to identify the nature of this extended structure, possible explanations include (i) companion/merging galaxies and (ii) massive AGN-driven outflows.
We naively attribute the core component in the double Gaussian fit to the host galaxy itself, hence providing its systemic redshift as z_ CII = 6.7224 0.0003 .

sources.summary: J1205 0000 is identified as a high-redshift red quasar whose SED is better fit by dust extinction than by a luminous host-galaxy contribution.

quotes.quote: J1205 0000: this is a z band dropout source and the y band flux is affected by intergalactic medium absorption, so we fitted the SED models to the VIKING J, H, Ks band fluxes and the decomposed W1, W2 band fluxes. The quasar plus extinction model with E(B-V) = 0.115^ +0.029 _ 0.027 gives a significantly better fit than the quasar plus galaxy model. In addition, the host galaxy inferred in the latter case ( M_ 1450 23.0 mag) is very luminous, given the characteristic magnitude of the z 7 galaxy luminosity function ( M^*_ 1450 20.8 mag; 2018PASJ...70S..10O ), but we found its HSC image being consistent with a point source, without apparent contribution from the host galaxy (this is however at the rest-frame 1400 , and the situation can be different at 4000--6000 \ traced by the WISE bands). We concluded that the quasar plus extinction model is more reasonable, and that this object is a red quasar.

gmw_00004616.name: MS 0331.3 3629
gmw_00004616.overview: MS 0331.3 3629 is a high-peaked BL Lacertae object and blazar candidate located at a redshift of z=0.308 [arXiv:0903.1548]. Optical observations reveal that the active nucleus is hosted by an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:0903.1548].

sources.summary: The appendix describes MS 0331.3 3629 as a z=0.308 high-peaked BL Lac candidate with Chandra/XMM spectra, an optical BL Lac classification, and an elliptical host.

quotes.quote: We present a series of high-quality (up to 2 10^4 counts) X-ray imaging spectra of the high-peaked BL Lac (HBL) MS 0331.3 3629. MS 0331.3 3629 (03:33:12.2, 36:19:48.0) is a z=0.308 blazar candidate Stocke91 which was serendipitously detected in six recent long exposures of NGC 1365 -- falling within one Chandra and five XMM-Newton fields. Table tabChandraXMM shows a summary of the recent Chandra and XMM-Newton observations of MS 0331.3 3629.

The optical spectrum of MS 0331.3 3629 shows no emission lines and has a Ca II H K break of 30 Stocke91 , consistent with a BL Lac classification at z=0.308 . An HST observation, taken on 1996 October 29, revealed an elliptical host which outshines the active nucleus at 7020 \ the host apparent magnitude is R=17.83 , compared to R=19.03 for the active nucleus, HST . Note that most prior work on the optical-to-X-ray and optical-to-radio flux ratios used the total galaxy magnitude in the optical, leading to incorrect estimates.

gmw_00004617.name: 4C 41.17
gmw_00004617.overview: At a redshift of approximately 3.8, 4C 41.17 is a massive, clumpy radio galaxy [arXiv:1301.1983, arXiv:2306.00071] surrounded by a giant Lyman-alpha emitting halo [arXiv:astro-ph/0212011, arXiv:2306.00071] in an overdense region of submillimeter-selected galaxies [arXiv:0707.4482]. The system features a double-double radio source [arXiv:astro-ph/0212011] with extended X-ray emission [arXiv:1702.04725, arXiv:2007.10368] and a narrow ridge of dust [arXiv:2006.10572].

sources.summary: The paper models 4C 41.17 as a z=3.8 radio galaxy whose SED requires a recent massive starburst plus an older stellar population, with mergers and jet-cloud interaction favored.

quotes.quote: Using the new evolutionary code .3, we made an evolutionary spectral synthesis of the optical-IR-submm spectral energy distribution of two distant ( z=3.8 ) radio galaxies, 4C 41.17 and TN J2007 1316. These two radio galaxies were selected from the HeRG E (Herschel Radio Galaxies Evolution) Project in particular for their faint AGN contribution and because they show evidence of a large stellar contribution to their bolometric luminosity. We chose two radio galaxies in particular, 4C 41.17 and TN J2007 1316, both at z=3.8, because of their small AGN contribution to the overall SED.

The radio galaxy 4C 41.17 ( z=3.800 0.003 ) is the archetype of distant radio galaxies ch90 . A detailed multi-frequency radio analysis with the VLA and MERLIN shows that the radio structure is associated with high resolution optical imaging HST data car94,mi92 . The galaxy is detected with a good signal-to-noise ratio and is spatially resolved at the 0.1'' HST resolution. Based on both the C _ III ] \ to C _ IV \ line ratio and the strength of the C _ IV \ line, bi00 suggested that there is an interaction of the high-powered jet with a dense cloud in the halo of 4C 41.17.

sources.summary: The paper models 4C41.17 as a z=3.8 radio galaxy whose cumulative supernova-remnant mass approaches SMBH scales and whose HST/VLA sub-kiloparsec structures link evolved stars with compact radio zones.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we show that the supernova remnant (SNR) masses cumulated from core-collapse supernovae along the star formation history of two powerful z=3.8 radio galaxies 4C41.17 and TN J2007-1316 reach up to 10^9 M_ , comparable with supermassive black hole (SMBH) masses measured from the SDSS sample at similar redshifts. The SNR mass is measured from the already exploded supernova mass after subtraction of ejecta while the mass of still luminous stars fits at best the observed spectral energy distribution (SED), continuously extended to the optical-Spitzer-Herschel-submm domains, with the help of the galaxy evolution model P egase.3. For the recent and old stellar populations, SNR masses vary on 10 ^ 9-10 M_ and the SNR-to-star mass ratio between 1 and 0.1 is comparable to the observed low-z SMBH-to-star mass ratio. For the template radio galaxy 4C41.17, SNR and stellar population masses estimated from large aperture ( > 4arcsec=30kpc) observations are compatible, within one mass order, with the total mass of multiple optical HST ( 700pc) structures, associated with VLA radio emissions, both at 0.1arcsec.

At higher resolution, the template radio galaxy 4C41.17 was observed with HST in the optical Mil92 and with VLA on multifrequency radio continuum, in particular at 8.3 GHz Car94 . At the same 0.1 arcsec resolution, multiple structures of 500pc size are identified. The remarkable similarity between the optical and radio morphologies of these structures is confirmed: the evolved stellar components as H1 to H4 are within the error bars, located on compact radio emission zones B1-B2-B3 with the core N between B1 and B2, see figure 1c and plate 55 of Car94 .

sources.summary: The paper observes 4C 41.17 as a z=3.8 radio galaxy and positive AGN feedback candidate, measuring dust, line emission, gas mass, and star formation near its jet.

quotes.quote: We present new, spatially resolved [ CI ] 1--0, [ CI ] 2--1, CO(7--6), and dust continuum observations of 4C 41.17 at z=3.8 . This is one of the best-studied radio galaxies in this epoch and is arguably the best candidate of jet-triggered star formation at high redshift currently known in the literature. 4C 41.17 shows a narrow ridge of dust continuum extending over 15 kpc near the radio jet axis.

Line emission is found within the galaxy in the region with signatures of positive feedback. Using the [ CI ] 1--0 line as a molecular gas tracer, and multifrequency observations of the far-infrared dust heated by star formation, we find a total gas mass of 7.6 10^ 10 M _ , which is somewhat greater than that previously found from CO(4--3). The gas mass surface density of 10^3 M _ yr ^ 1 pc ^ 2 and the star formation rate surface density of 10 M _ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 were derived over the 12 kpc 8 kpc area, where signatures of positive feedback have previously been found. These densities are comparable to those in other populations of massive, dusty star-forming galaxies in this redshift range, suggesting that the jet does not currently enhance the efficiency with which stars form from the gas.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C41.17 as a z=3.798 radio galaxy with a very large Ly-alpha nebula, filamentary enriched gas, and twisted radio structure indicating entrainment by the radio source.

quotes.quote: 4C41.17 at z = 3.798 4C41.17 was imaged using a custom--made, high throughput interference filter with a 65 \ bandpass centered at the redshifted \ = 5839 \ line with the Echellette Spectrograph and Imager at the Cassegrain focus of the Keck II 10m telescope. The data were obtained during photometric conditions and good seeing (FWHM = 0.57 ). With a total exposure time of 27,600 s this \ image is the most sensitive obtained to date and reaches a surface brightness of 8.0 10^ 19 erg s^ 1 cm^ 2 arcsec^ 2 (3 limit in a 2.0 \ diameter aperture). A broad--band R image, obtained as part of a multi--band spectral energy distribution study of the 4C41.17 field was scaled and subtracted from the \ image to construct the pure emission--line image shown in Figure 1. With linear dimensions of 190 130 at the detection limit the 4C41.17 nebula is the largest presently known (Table 1).

The kinematics along the filament in 4C41.17, its radial filamentary structure, and the chemical enrichment of this gas therefore all indicate a process of entrainment of material away from the central regions by the radio source.

The multiple component, asymmetric, and twisted radio structure of 4C41.17 and many other \ suggests that the central SMBH may experience multiple periods of radio source activity and precession, presumably triggered by the interaction with one of the many clumpy components which make up the complex galaxy structure.

sources.summary: The paper finds statistically significant extended X-ray emission around 4C 41.17, aligned with radio structure, and separates extended and point-source counts for CMB-quenching analysis.

quotes.quote: 4C 41.17 has been observed twice by , yielding a total (good) exposure time of 137 ks. The upper left panel of Fig. 4c41_fig shows the merged, full-band \ image, where the green open circle labels the position of the point \ source as identified by +wavdetect+. To inspect the source spatial profile, we first created a sub-pixel full-band \ image for 4C 41.17, shown in the upper right panel of Fig. 4c41_fig .

A direct comparison of the radial profile of the source photon density profile for 4C 41.17 (solid line) against the simulated PSF's (dashed line), shown in Fig. 4c41_ctsden_fig , confirms the presence of a statistically significant extended component that goes well beyond 2 off the central point source in 4C 41.17. The measured photon density outside the central 2 declines following roughly an exponential law, and becomes indistinguishable from the background level beyond 8 . To estimate the total \ counts within the extended emission, we fit an exponential law (corresponding to a linear relation if the photon density is in log-scale) between 2 and 8 , and then extrapolated it into inside 2 , yielding a total of 111.9^ +11.6 _ 10.6 counts over the full energy band.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C 41.17, the Anthill, as a z=3.8 massive clumpy radio galaxy whose properties resemble the Spiderweb and indicate a forming brightest cluster galaxy.

quotes.quote: 4C 41.17 The Anthill : The 4C41.17 radio source is associated with a spectacular massive galaxy at redshift z=3.8 miley2008distant . It was discovered because of its high radio luminosity and ultra-steep spectral index by chambers19904c . Deep imaging with the HST revealed that the host galaxy is a spectacularly large clumpy group of faint galaxies that appear to be merging with the massive host galaxy.

In several of its properties, the Anthill closely resembles the well-studied Spiderweb Galaxy/protocluster at z=2.2 miley2008distant . Both are among the most massive, clumpiest, and largest galaxies known, consistent with cD galaxies in the process of formation. Both are surrounded by giant Lya-emitting ionized halos whose sizes exceed 100 kpc.

sources.summary: The 4C 41.17 field is the richest 350 micron field in the sample, with multiple SHARC-II detections and evidence for an overdensity of submillimeter sources.

quotes.quote: 4C 41.17 With 5 sources detected at 5 significance, this is by far the richest field observed at 350- m. All three scuba sources detected by Stevens et al., including the RG which even appears to be slightly extended, are detected at 350- m, and their flux densities are listed in Table 1.

The most significant ( 8 ) 350- m source lies 53 south-west of the RG, and although it is not detected at 850- m it appears to be associated with the very bright scuba (and sharc-ii ) source 30 north-east of it. The two 350- m sources make up a large, extended structure aligned along the jet-direction. The reality of this structure, and of the most south-western source in particular, will have to be confirmed by independent submm observations.

Although, we cannot exclude that some of the sharc-ii sources (in particular the ones closest to the map edges) are spurious, the confirmation of the three scuba sources reported by Stevens et al. \ (2003), supports their conclusions that the environment of 4C 41.17 has an overdensity of submm sources.

sources.summary: The paper describes 4C 41.17 as a z=3.8 radio galaxy whose double-double radio source, Ly halo, jet-induced star formation, and outer-jet outflow exemplify radio-jet feedback.

quotes.quote: A fine example of such a source is provided by the z 3.8 radio galaxy 4C 41.17 which has recently been studied in detail by Bicknell00 . This object consists of a powerful ``double-double'' radio source embedded in a 190 130 kpc Ly halo Reuland03 and shows strong evidence for jet-induced star formation at 3000 M_ yr^ 1 associated with the inner radio jet. This is apparently induced by the strong dynamical interaction of the inner jet with the shocked and compressed gas in the wall of the cocoon created by the passage of the outer jet.

In 4C 41.17, the outer jet also appears to have induced a large-scale outflow with velocities in excess of 500 km s ^ 1 in the line-emitting gaseous halo. Thus we may be seeing the ``end of the beginning'' in which the central supermassive black hole has finally become large enough to drive the whole accreting envolope of gas into outflow, triggering a last and spectacular burst of star formation in the process.

sources.summary: The paper presents submillimeter imaging of the high-redshift radio galaxy 4C 41.17, detects it at 350 and 1200 microns, and places it in an overdense field of SMGs.

quotes.quote: We present deep 350- and 1200- m imaging of the region around 4C 41.17 -- one of the most distant ( z = 3.792) and luminous known radio galaxies -- obtained with the Submillimeter High Angular Resolution Camera (SHARC-II) and the Max Planck Millimeter Bolometer Array (MAMBO). The radio galaxy is robustly detected at 350- and 1200- m, as are two nearby 850- m-selected galaxies; a third 850- m source is detected at 350- m and coincides with a 2 feature in the 1200- m map. Further away from the radio galaxy an additional nine sources are detected at 1200- m, bringing the total number of detected (sub)millimeter selected galaxies (SMGs) in this field to 14.

We thus confirm that 4C 41.17 resides in an over-dense region of the Universe, but we have only been able to identify SMGs along the line of sight to the radio galaxy, typical of the blank-field SMG population.

sources.summary: The paper treats 4C 41.17 as a comparison z>3.5 radio galaxy with extended X-ray emission and hotspot/lobe energy-density behavior in jet SED modelling.

quotes.quote: Although we considered admittedly extreme either-or scenarios, the results from this modelling exercise are not incompatible with the conclusions drawn by wu17 , who followed the same approach in modelling the SED of 4C 41.17 and 4C 03.24, i.e. the two other (known) z > 3.5 radio galaxies with (reliably) extended X-ray emission. For both systems, the energy density in magnetic field was found to vastly exceed that of the CMB radiation in the hotspots (whereas the opposite is true for the extended lobes). This implies that, in both systems, most of the X-ray signal is likely produced within the lobes, via IC/CMB, whereas most of the radio signal is produced within the hotspots, via synchrotron, thus washing out the steep (1+z)^4 enhancement of the X-ray vs. radio luminosity expected of IC/CMB.

gmw_00004618.name: GRG1
gmw_00004618.overview: GRG1 is a giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.433 [arXiv:1704.00516]. It is a low surface brightness radio source with a projected linear size of approximately 2.4 Mpc and features two radio lobes flanking a faint core [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG1 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG1 (J001604+042024) GRG1 is a very low surface brightness radio source ( 50 mJy) with a projected linear size of about 2.4 Mpc. The radio maps from NVSS show two radio lobes on both sides of the faint core. The host galaxy is at redshift of 0.433 and is one of the distant GRGs in our sample.

gmw_00004619.name: GRG2
gmw_00004619.overview: GRG2 is a giant radio galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of 0.571 [arXiv:1704.00516]. It has a projected linear size of 2.5 Mpc, and radio observations resolve its faint radio core and two radio lobes [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG2 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG2 (J002224.96-081845) GRG2 is the fourth most distant GRG in our reporting sample with a spectroscopic redshift of 0.571. This results in an overall projected linear size of 2.5 Mpc. A fewer fraction of GRGs with overall size greater than 2 Mpc are known and even fewer at higher redshifts. It is the most powerful source from our sample as shown in Table basicinfo . The FIRST map clearly resolves the faint radio core and the two radio lobes.

gmw_00004620.name: GRG4
gmw_00004620.overview: GRG4 is a giant radio galaxy located at a moderately high redshift of approximately 0.47 [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its host galaxy exhibits a red color that may be attributed to dust obscuration, and it displays a very high infrared luminosity [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG4 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG4 (J042925+003304): A Giant Radio Quasar? We observe GRG4 to have a very high infrared luminosity as seen in Table wise . GRG4 is located at a moderately high redshift of z 0.47 (SDSS-photoZ). Figure optical shows the host galaxy exhibiting a prominent red colour which could be attributed to obscuration from dust. This could be a possible reason why this galaxy has acquired such a large infrared luminosity.

gmw_00004621.name: GRG5
gmw_00004621.overview: GRG5 is a giant radio quasar with a spectroscopic redshift of 0.315 [arXiv:1704.00516]. It features a massive central black hole, a strong core-dominated radio structure, and double lobes that span a projected linear size of 2.1 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG5 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG5 (J044932-302638): A Giant Radio Quasar GRG5 has a strong core dominated feature along with a double lobe structure as seen in Figure subfig-5 . The southern lobe is possibly contaminated by another unrelated radio source as it shows an apparent extension towards the western side. This object is also observed in the SUMSS which is slightly poorer in resolution and sensitivity when compared to NVSS. The spectroscopic redshift of 0.315 was obtained from 2009MNRAS.399..683J from which the projected linear size is 2.1 Mpc. We infer the central black hole to be massive at 1.7 10^ 9 M_ (Table mass ).

gmw_00004622.name: GRG6
gmw_00004622.overview: GRG6 is a giant radio galaxy with a projected linear size of approximately 1.88 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516]. Located at a redshift of 0.678, its radio core and lobes are clearly resolved in FIRST mapping [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG6 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG6 (J080248+492723) GRG6 is about 1.88 Mpc in projected linear size with its host galaxy at a redshift of 0.678. This is the most distant GRG in our sample. Not many GRGs of this size are known at higher redshifts. The FIRST map clearly resolves the radio core and lobes. The black hole mass of the host is unknown due to non-availability of K band and SDSS data.

gmw_00004623.name: GRG7
gmw_00004623.overview: GRG7 is a giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.27 [arXiv:1601.00003]. It exhibits a Fanaroff-Riley type II radio morphology with a core and two lobes [arXiv:1704.00516], spanning a projected linear size of 1.3 to 1.6 megaparsecs [arXiv:1601.00003, arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper uses GAMAJ085701.76+013130.9 as a GAMA/GMRT match whose radio contours reveal a z=0.27, 1.3 Mpc, previously undiscovered giant radio galaxy with core-lobe matching challenges.

quotes.quote: Figure  GRG shows an example of one of the GAMA/GMRT matches, GAMAJ085701.76+013130.9, with the SDSS band image overlaid with radio contours from the GMRT (blue), FIRST (yellow) and NVSS (red). At z =0.27 this galaxy has a projected size of 1.3 Mpc and is a previously undiscovered Giant Radio Galaxy GRG; Schoenmakers2001, Saripalli2005 . Automated methods such as the likelihood ratio technique in this case would be able to match the core of the radio source to an optical counterpart, but would incorrectly match the lobes to some other optical source, if considered separately.

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG7 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG7 (J085701.76+013130.9) GRG7 has a very low surface brightness with an overall linear size of 1.6 Mpc. The FIRST map clearly resolves the radio core and the two radio lobes thus confirming its FR-II type nature. Also, the host galaxy is located 3.5 away from the GMBCG J134.30444+01.56066 galaxy cluster (which is at similar redshift as that of the GRG) as listed in the GMBCG catalog of galaxy clusters 2010ApJS..191..254H .

gmw_00004624.name: GRG8
gmw_00004624.overview: GRG8, also known as RGZ J105838.6+244535, is a giant radio galaxy with a projected size of 2.1 to 2.3 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516, arXiv:1711.09611]. Its northern lobe features a strong hotspot-like component [arXiv:1711.09611], while its southern lobe is highly diffuse and relaxed [arXiv:1704.00516, arXiv:1711.09611].

sources.summary: The paper identifies RGZ J105838.6+244535 as a 2.3 Mpc giant radio galaxy whose northwest lobe has a FIRST hotspot-like component and southeast lobe appears in NVSS.

quotes.quote: RGZ J105838.6+244535 (Figure  rys:hymors-large b): In the FIRST image the NW lobe displays a strong hotspot-like component at its far end. The SE lobe is resolved out in FIRST, but its diffuse emission is detected in the lower resolution NVSS image. There is no compact component in the SE lobe, and its overall structure seems relaxed. A compact radio core is detected in the FIRST image. This is a giant radio galaxy, with a size of 2.3 Mpc .

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG8 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG8 (J105838.66+244535) GRG8 shows a peculiar morphology extending over 2.1 Mpc. The southern lobe is highly diffuse with an integrated flux ( S_ i ) of just 30 mJy at 1.4 GHz. In contrast to the southern lobe, the northern lobe shows a typical edge brightened lobe with an integrated flux ( S_ i ) of 120 mJy.

gmw_00004625.name: GRG10
gmw_00004625.overview: GRG10 is a giant radio galaxy hosted by the optical galaxy SDSS J132741.32+574943.4 [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its radio core exhibits an extension toward the southern lobe that may represent an unresolved jet, while diffuse emission is visible beyond the northern lobe's hotspot [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG10 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG10 (J132741+574943) The radio core of GRG10 shows an extension towards the southern lobe which could be attributed to an unresolved radio jet. Interestingly, from Figure subfig-10 , we observe diffuse emission beyond the hotspot of the northern lobe. The radio core as detected in FIRST coincides with the galaxy (SDSS J132741.32+574943.4) thus confirming it as a host.

gmw_00004626.name: GRG11
gmw_00004626.overview: GRG11 is a distant giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.657 [arXiv:1704.00516]. Radio observations resolve the galaxy as a Fanaroff-Riley II type with distinctive lobes, and its host galaxy appears highly reddened [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG11 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG11 (J132743.50+174837) GRG11 is one of the more distant GRGs in our reporting sample with a redshift of 0.657. The FIRST map (Figure subfig-11 ) resolves GRG11 to be FR-II type with distinctive lobes. The host galaxy appears highly reddened (Figure optical ).

gmw_00004627.name: GRG12
gmw_00004627.overview: GRG12 is a giant radio galaxy with a strong, edge-brightened FR-II structure [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its host galaxy is a faint SDSS source with a photometric redshift of 0.412, indicating a projected linear size of 3.7 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper presents GRG12 as the largest source in its sample, a strong FR-II radio galaxy whose faint host implies a projected size of 3.7 Mpc.

quotes.quote: GRG12 (J200843+004918): An Extremely Large Radio Source? GRG12 has angular size of 11 showing a strong edge brightened FR-II structure. A faint galaxy SDSS J200843.37+004918.8 ( m_ = 20.76 ) coincides with the faint radio core as seen in Figure subfig-12 , indicating it to be the host galaxy of GRG12. The host galaxy has a photometric redshift of 0.412 (SDSS-photoZ) which results in 3.7 Mpc as its linear size. This would make GRG12 the largest GRG in the present sample as well as one of the largest radio sources known till date, after GRG J0422+1512 ( 4 Mpc) 2015AstBu..70...45A , 3C236 ( 4.6 Mpc) 1974Natur.250..625W and GRG J1420-0545, the biggest with a projected linear size of 4.9 Mpc.

gmw_00004628.name: GRG13
gmw_00004628.overview: GRG13 is a giant radio galaxy associated with the host galaxy 6dF J203449-263036 at a spectroscopic redshift of 0.103 [arXiv:1704.00516]. It features a very bright radio core, diffuse emission, and a highly bent south-western lobe [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG13 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG13 (J203449-263036) As seen in Figure subfig-13 , GRG13 does not display clear lobe hotspots. The emission around the core is more of diffuse nature. The radio core is very bright which coincides with the host galaxy (6dF J203449-263036) and has a spectroscopic redshift of 0.103 as obtained from 2009MNRAS.399..683J . GRG13 shows highly bent nature in the south-western lobe as compared to the north-eastern lobe. Due to presence of another strong radio source close to north-eastern lobe, the weak diffuse emission in this region is not very clearly seen in the NVSS map.

gmw_00004629.name: GRG14
gmw_00004629.overview: GRG14 is a giant radio galaxy with an asymmetric morphology [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its southern lobe features a strong hotspot surrounded by diffuse emission and is closer to the radio core than the northern lobe, which may be due to the local intergalactic medium [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG14 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG14 (J205939+243423) GRG14 shows an asymmetric morphology as evidenced in Figure subfig-14 . The southern lobe ( S_ i =58 mJy) shows a strong hotspot surrounded by some extended diffuse emission and is closer to the radio core than the northern lobe ( S_ i =23 mJy). This asymmetry could be attributed to the existence of varied IGM near the southern lobe. The host galaxy, as seen in Figure optical , shows slight elongated morphology. Spectrum and spectroscopic redshifts are not available for the host (SDSS J205939.82+243423.9) of GRG14.

gmw_00004630.name: GRG16
gmw_00004630.overview: GRG16 is a giant radio galaxy with a projected linear size of 0.9 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its radio core perfectly coincides with its optical host galaxy, which is located at a redshift of 0.097 and exhibits asymmetric hotspots with diffuse emission [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG16 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG16 (J225039.15+284445.5) The radio core coincides with the optical host (SDSS J225039.15+284445.5) perfectly which is at a redshift of 0.097 thus making its projected linear size as 0.9 Mpc. In Figure subfig-16 , we see the evidence of the source showing hotspots which are asymmetric. We also observe diffuse emission beyond the hotspots.

gmw_00004631.name: GRG17
gmw_00004631.overview: GRG17 is a giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of 0.09 [arXiv:1704.00516]. It has a projected linear size of 1.5 Mpc and exhibits a highly diffuse radio morphology, featuring a southern lobe with very low surface brightness and a northern lobe with a potential hotspot [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG17 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG17 (J225615.1-361759) GRG17 shows highly diffuse nature with its southern lobe more diffuse than the northern one (Figure subfig-17 ). The northern lobe has some hint of a hotspot while the southern one is highly diffuse and has a very low surface brightness. The redshift as obtained from 2009MNRAS.399..683J , is 0.09 which makes its projected linear size as 1.5 Mpc.

gmw_00004632.name: GRG18
gmw_00004632.overview: GRG18 is a giant radio galaxy with a linear projected size of approximately 0.9 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its radio core coincides with the optical host galaxy, and it exhibits a low surface brightness without clear hotspots [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG18 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG18 (J230444-105048) GRG18 has low surface brightness and does not show clear hotspots, as seen from Figure subfig-18 . Its radio core is seen to coincide with optical host (2MASX J23044483-1050474). The redshift, as obtained from 2009MNRAS.399..683J , is about 0.21 which results in its linear projected size to be 0.9 Mpc.

gmw_00004633.name: GRG19
gmw_00004633.overview: GRG19 is a giant radio galaxy with a projected linear size of 1.7 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its radio core coincides with an optical host galaxy at a redshift of 0.140, displaying an asymmetric morphology where the southern lobe is brighter than the northern lobe [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG19 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG19 (J231201.27+135655.9) The radio core is seen to coincide with the optical host (SDSS J231201.27+135655.9) and is at a redshift of 0.140 which results in its projected linear size of 1.7 Mpc. In Figure subfig-19 , we observe the southern lobe to be brighter than the northern lobe. The northern lobe shows a hotspot towards the far end.

gmw_00004634.name: GRG20
gmw_00004634.overview: GRG20 is a giant radio galaxy with a projected linear size of 1.5 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its radio core is off-centered towards the eastern lobe and coincides with the optical host galaxy, which lies in the QSO region of the WISE color-color diagram [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG20 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG20 (J231620-010207) In Figure subfig-20 , we see the distinct lobes of GRG20 with the radio core off centered towards the eastern lobe. The radio core, as seen in FIRST, coincides with the optical host (SDSS J231620.15-010207.3). At a redshift of 0.221 (SDSS photoZ) gives its projected linear size is 1.5 Mpc. Through the WISE colour-colour diagram we observe it to lie in the QSO region. Further optical studies are required to confirm the true nature of the host galaxy.

gmw_00004635.name: GRG21
gmw_00004635.overview: GRG21 is a giant radio galaxy with an extremely large projected size of 2.9 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516]. Based on mid-infrared colors, it is classified as a high-excitation radio galaxy hosting a central black hole with a mass of approximately 2.2 billion solar masses [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG21 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG21 (J232623+245840) Having an extremely large size of 2.9 Mpc, GRG21 is the third largest in our reporting sample. We obtained the spectroscopic redshift (0.2549) and velocity dispersion ( = 202.82 14.14 km/s) from SDSS DR12 spectrum. Using the M_ BH relation we computed the mass (Table msigma-mbh ) of the central black hole to be 0.22 0.08 10^ 9 M_ . It has a high core radio luminosity ( L_ ) = 2 10^ 25 W Hz^ 1 computed from the NVSS maps. Based on the criteria mentioned in Section 2.2 and mid-IR colour-colour (Figure ccplot ) plot, we classify it to be a HERG.

gmw_00004636.name: GRG22
gmw_00004636.overview: GRG22 is a giant radio galaxy with a projected linear size of 1.9 Mpc at a redshift of 0.555 [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its radio morphology is highly asymmetric, featuring a resolved radio core that lies towards the northern lobe [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG22 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG22 (J232849.99-082512.7) At a redshift 0.555 its measured projected linear size is 1.9 Mpc, where the host galaxy (SDSS J232849.99-082512.7) coincides with the radio core perfectly. From Figure subfig-22 we observe that GRG 22 is highly asymmetric and the radio core lies towards the northern lobe. The core is clearly resolved in FIRST and can be seen from the black contours plotted in Figure subfig-22 . The black contours seen in Figure subfig-22 are from FIRST where the core is clearly resolved. This GRG is also mentioned in 2015salt.confE..34B where they have obtained the spectroscopic redshift of the host galaxy which is lesser than that of SDSS as quoted above.

gmw_00004637.name: GRG23
gmw_00004637.overview: GRG23 is an asymmetric giant radio galaxy with a projected linear size of 0.85 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516]. Its host galaxy, 2MASX J233552.14+521538.7, is located at a redshift of 0.07 and coincides with the radio core [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG23 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG23 (J233552.1+521539) The host 2MASX J233552.14+521538.7 coincides with the radio core and is observed to be towards the north-eastern lobe. With a redshift of 0.07 2012ApJS..199...26H , its projected linear size measures 0.85 Mpc. From Figure subfig-23 , we observe GRG23 to be an asymmetric GRG with the south-western lobe being very elongated and showing a hotspot.

gmw_00004638.name: GRG24
gmw_00004638.overview: GRG24 is a giant radio galaxy with a host galaxy, SDSS J234929.77-000305.8, that coincides with its radio core [arXiv:1704.00516]. Located at a redshift of 0.187, the galaxy has a projected linear size of 0.84 Mpc and features an elongated western lobe alongside a diffuse eastern lobe [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRG24 as a giant radio galaxy and describes its host, projected size, radio morphology, and available redshift or AGN classification information.

quotes.quote: GRG24 (J234929-000305) The host galaxy (SDSS J234929.77-000305.8) for GRG24 is seen to coincide with the radio core which is prominently seen in FIRST. With a redshift of 0.187 its projected linear size measures 0.84 Mpc. From Figure subfig-24 , we observe the eastern lobe to show further diffuse emission while the western lobe is elongated.

gmw_00004639.name: GRG25
gmw_00004639.overview: GRG25 is a giant radio galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.657 [arXiv:1704.00516]. It exhibits a double-lobed radio morphology with a host galaxy that coincides with its radio core, yielding a projected linear size of 3.5 Mpc [arXiv:1704.00516].

sources.summary: The paper reports GRG25 as the second-largest and more distant sample member, with a double-lobed morphology, SDSS host, and projected size of 3.5 Mpc.

quotes.quote: GRG25 (J235531+025607) GRG25 is the second largest GRG and one of the more distant from our reporting sample. The SDSS photoZ is of redshift 0.657 thus giving the projected linear size as 3.5 Mpc. There are scarcely any GRGs known at this redshift with sizes greater than 2 Mpc. We observe the host galaxy (SDSS J235531.63+025607.1) to coincide with the radio core. This GRG shows a double lobed morphology, as evidenced from Figure subfig-25 .

gmw_00004640.name: MJM 7
gmw_00004640.overview: MJM 7 is a quasar characterized by a steep fitted X-ray photon index [arXiv:astro-ph/0208191]. Observations reveal that the source exhibits soft X-ray absorption in excess of the Galactic column density along its line of sight [arXiv:astro-ph/0208191].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MJM 7 as a QSO with excess soft X-ray absorption above the Galactic column and a steep fitted photon index in ROSAT PSPC spectra.

quotes.quote: The absorbing column densities, 20.7_ 9.7 ^ +13.6 10^ 19 cm^ 2 , 18.1_ 4.7 ^ +6.0 10^ 19 cm^ 2 , 18.7_ 7.4 ^ +9.8 10^ 19 cm^ 2 , derived for the QSOs MJM 7, MJM 10, and MJM 15 by fitting absorbed power-law model are in excess of the Galactic value ( 7.9 10^ 19 cm^ 2 ) measured along their line of sight. This indicates that all the X-ray absorption may not be only due to matter in our own Galaxy but also due to matter local to the source. In order to verify the presence of excess absorption, we fixed the neutral hydrogen column density to the Galactic value and fitted the absorbed power-law model (Model B).

To estimate the excess absorption local to each of the three QSOs, we introduced an additional component for absorption local to the source in our model B (Model C -- redshifted power-law modified with Galactic as well as intrinsic absorption local to the source) and fitted to PSPC spectrum of the each of the three QSOs. The derived excess absorption, N_H , are 9.7_ 8.1 ^ +12.0 10^ 20 cm^ 2 for MJM 7, 2.8_ 0.8 ^ +1.7 10^ 20 cm^ 2 for MJM 10, and 9.5_ 7.4 ^ +10.0 10^ 20 cm^ 2 for MJM 15. The best-fit photon indices ( _ X ) are 3.1_ 0.6 ^ +0.8 , 3.7_ 0.3 ^ +0.3 , and 3.3_ 0.5 ^ +0.7 for MJM 7, MJM 10, and MJM 15, respectively.

gmw_00004641.name: MJM 10
gmw_00004641.overview: MJM 10 is a quasar that exhibits a steep fitted photon index in its ROSAT PSPC spectra [arXiv:astro-ph/0208191]. The source displays excess soft X-ray absorption above the Galactic column, indicating the presence of absorbing matter local to the quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0208191].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MJM 10 as a QSO with excess soft X-ray absorption above the Galactic column and a steep fitted photon index in ROSAT PSPC spectra.

The derived excess absorption, N_H , are 9.7_ 8.1 ^ +12.0 10^ 20 cm^ 2 for MJM 7, 2.8_ 0.8 ^ +1.7 10^ 20 cm^ 2 for MJM 10, and 9.5_ 7.4 ^ +10.0 10^ 20 cm^ 2 for MJM 15. The best-fit photon indices ( _ X ) are 3.1_ 0.6 ^ +0.8 , 3.7_ 0.3 ^ +0.3 , and 3.3_ 0.5 ^ +0.7 for MJM 7, MJM 10, and MJM 15, respectively.

gmw_00004642.name: MJM 15
gmw_00004642.overview: MJM 15 is a quasar that exhibits a steep fitted photon index in its ROSAT PSPC spectra [arXiv:astro-ph/0208191]. The source displays excess soft X-ray absorption above the Galactic column, indicating the presence of absorbing matter local to the quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0208191].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MJM 15 as a QSO with excess soft X-ray absorption above the Galactic column and a steep fitted photon index in ROSAT PSPC spectra.

quotes.quote: The absorbing column densities, 20.7_ 9.7 ^ +13.6 10^ 19 cm^ 2 , 18.1_ 4.7 ^ +6.0 10^ 19 cm^ 2 , 18.7_ 7.4 ^ +9.8 10^ 19 cm^ 2 , derived for the QSOs MJM 7, MJM 10, and MJM 15 by fitting absorbed power-law model are in excess of the Galactic value ( 7.9 10^ 19 cm^ 2 ) measured along their line of sight. The best-fit photon indices ( _ X ) are 3.1_ 0.6 ^ +0.8 , 3.7_ 0.3 ^ +0.3 , and 3.3_ 0.5 ^ +0.7 for MJM 7, MJM 10, and MJM 15, respectively.

gmw_00004643.name: FDF0809
gmw_00004643.overview: FDF0809 is a galaxy located at a redshift of 0.8650 [arXiv:0807.1091]. Its host galaxy is resolved across multiple filters and exhibits a disk-type morphology with an effective radius of approximately 1.5 to 4 kpc [arXiv:0807.1091].

sources.summary: The paper resolves the host galaxy of FDF0809 in all filters, finding disk-type morphology and effective radii of roughly 1.5-4 kpc from image decomposition.

quotes.quote: FDF0809 ( z=0.8650 ): This is the object with the lowest redshift, the host galaxy was resolved in all filters. Error simulations yielded resolution with 5 significance in six out of eight filters and resolution with 3 significance in the two other filters. Thus the host galaxy is clearly resolved and has a disk type morphology. The radii found are in the range 0.''2 - 0.''5 , corresponding to 1.5 - 4 kpc. The radii are significantly smaller than the mean seeing, still, they are all larger than one pixel, thus we consider the galaxy as resolved.

gmw_00004644.name: FDF1837
gmw_00004644.overview: FDF1837 is a high-redshift quasar host galaxy candidate located at a redshift of z=2.2540 [arXiv:0807.1091]. The host galaxy was formally detected in the U, B, g, R, and I filters, though its resolution remains uncertain due to sub-pixel effective radii [arXiv:0807.1091].

sources.summary: The paper treats FDF1837 as a questionable quasar host detection: formally detected in several filters, but limited by sub-pixel radii and uncertain resolution.

quotes.quote: FDF1837 ( z=2.2540 ): A host was formally detected in the filters UBgRI. According to the error simulations, the object is resolved in the filters B (5 ) and I (3 ). With effective radii ranging from 0.''11 - 0.''30 and a pixel scale of the chip of 0.''2 we would like to point out that at least for the filters R and I the galaxy doesn't seem resolved. Taking the error bars for r_ e into account we are on the limit of rejecting the host galaxy detection due to sub-pixel host galaxy radii. In this situation, even a 5 significance is not a strong argument for the host galaxy being resolved.

gmw_00004645.name: FDF2229
gmw_00004645.overview: FDF2229 is a high-redshift galaxy at a redshift of z=2.1560 [arXiv:0807.1091]. Its host galaxy is considered possibly resolved, displaying very small effective radii in the red bands [arXiv:0807.1091].

sources.summary: The paper reports a formal host detection for FDF2229 but considers the host only possibly resolved, with very small red-band radii and upper-limit magnitudes.

quotes.quote: FDF2229 ( z=2.1560 ): A host was formally detected in the filters UBgRI. The error simulations yielded a 3 significance in the U and B-band and a 5 significance in the g-band. As for FDF1837, red bands here show very small effective radii, we therefore follow the arguments used for FDF1837. We consider the host galaxy as possibly resolved. The magnitudes are considered to be upper limits.

gmw_00004646.name: FDF6007
gmw_00004646.overview: FDF6007 is a high-redshift quasar host galaxy located at a redshift of z=2.7515 [arXiv:0807.1091]. The host galaxy has been resolved in the B, g, R, and I photometric bands, although it remains too faint for successful fits in the U, J, and Ks bands [arXiv:0807.1091].

sources.summary: The paper resolves the high-redshift quasar host FDF6007 in several bands, but notes faintness, Ly contamination in B, and uncertainties in the host-galaxy fit.

quotes.quote: FDF6007 ( z=2.7515 ): This is the object with the highest redshift where we could resolve the host galaxy. The object was formally resolved in the filters BgRI. No fits were possible in the filters UJKs due to the object being too faint. According to the error simulations, the object was resolved with 5 significance in R and I and with 3 significance in B. However, Ly lies in the B band, thus the same argument holds as for FDF1837 and FDF2229.

gmw_00004647.name: SDSS J103516.20+142200.6
gmw_00004647.overview: SDSS J103516.20+142200.6 is a redshifted broad-absorption line quasar located at a redshift of approximately 1.2528 [arXiv:1909.07582]. The active nucleus is powered by a supermassive black hole that is directly fed by dense, inflowing gas [arXiv:1909.07582].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J103516.20+142200.6 as the archetypal redshifted-BAL quasar, modeling dense inflowing gas near its SMBH as direct fuel for the accretion disk.

quotes.quote: Extensive photo-ionization modeling for the archetypical object SDSS J103516.20+142200.6 indicates the inflowing gas to be dense, thick and moderately ionized, with a characteristic distance to the SMBH of 1,000 gravitational radii, possibly overlapping or close to the outer accretion disk. Our results present the first compelling evidence for the long-sought inflow directly feeding quasars' accretion disks with external materials, likely originating from the dusty torus at a parsec scale.

In the rest of this paper, we performed further detailed analysis for J1035+1422 ( z=1.2528 0.0001 ) as demonstration, which has the highest data quality among the sample. The quasar has a mass accretion rate M _ 13 M_ yr ^ 1 derived from its luminosity using the standard bolometric correction ( L_ bol 8.1 L_ 5100 ) assuming an accretion efficiency = 0.1 , and a size of the broad emission line region (BELR) R_ BELR 0.33 pc derived from the R_ BELR -luminosity relation . We estimated the black hole mass M_ 2 10^ 10 M_ from the parameters of the reconstructed Balmer BEL and the size of the BELR, with an uncertainty of 0.5 dex .

We thus consider the redshifted BALs in J1035+1422, and in other quasars of our sample in general, to be compelling evidence for the long-sought inflows that directly feed the accretion disks and consequently the SMBHs. Our result demonstrates that the accretion disks in quasars are supplied with external gas transported inward from a distant inventory, at least in some objects. Interestingly enough, the outer distance range of the inflow 3.2  pc 3.3 10^ 3 R_ g is comparable to the radius of the postulated dusty torus, which was evaluated by the observed luminosity as the dust sublimation distance R_ sub 3.0 3.1 10^ 3 R_ g .

gmw_00004648.name: PKS 1549--79
gmw_00004648.overview: PKS 1549--79 is an obscured, radio-loud quasar caught in the early stages of an ongoing galaxy merger [arXiv:2005.04765, arXiv:1910.07865]. The system features a young, stable radio jet [arXiv:2005.04765, arXiv:2007.10474] that drives a massive, compact molecular gas outflow within its circumnuclear disk [arXiv:1910.07865, arXiv:2201.04157].

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1549--79 as an obscured far-IR bright quasar with a young radio jet, merger-fed gas structures, a circumnuclear disk, and a compact massive molecular outflow.

quotes.quote: The first object shown here is \ ( z = 0.150 ), having a young radio jet ( 300 pc in size) hosted by an obscured, far-IR bright quasar and, therefore, in a particularly crucial early stage in the evolution Holt06 . As expected, a fast outflow of warm ionised gas as well as a Ultra Fast Outflow in X-rays Tombesi14 are present, but the kinetic power of the warm outflow 4 10^ 4 L_ edd . The \ and CO(3-2) ALMA high resolution observations ( Oosterloo19 ) show the presence of three gas structures, which can be seen in Fig. \ fig:fig1 . Kiloparsec-scale tails are observed, resulting from an on-going merger which provide gas that is accreting onto the centre of PKS 1549--79.

At the same time, a circum-nuclear disc has formed in the inner few hundred parsec, and a very broad ( > 2300 ) component associated with fast outflowing molecular gas is detected at the position of the AGN. As expected, the outflow is massive ( 600 ) but, despite the fact that \ should represent an ideal case of feedback in action, it is limited to the inner 200 pc. These results illustrate that the impact on the surrounding medium of the energy released by the AGN is not always as expected from the feedback scenario. The outflow of warm, ionised gas is slightly more extended (see Oosterloo19 ), but modest in terms of mass outflow rate ( 2 ; Holt06 , Santoro et al. in prep).

sources.summary: The paper includes PKS 1549--79 as an intrinsically compact young radio source whose emission-line fitting and HST data indicate warm ionized gas dominated by outflowing material.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, PKS 1549--79 was included since, although it is a highly asymmetric radio source with a bright, flat-spectrum core, there is evidence that its radio structures are intrinsically compact, rather than just appearing compact as a result of its jets pointing close to our line of sight see discussion in Holt2006,Oosterloo2019 .

HST observations In order to determine the extents of their warm outflows, three of the sources in our sample --- PKS 0023--26, PKS 1306--09, and PKS 1549--79 --- were observed using the High Resolution Camera (HRC) or Wide Field Camera (WFC) of the Advanced Camera for Surveys (ACS) mounted on the HST. The reduction and analysis of the ACS/HRC \ imaging observations for PKS 1549--79 is presented in Batcheldor2007 , and further analysis and discussion on the relationship between the \ and radio structures in this source is presented in Oosterloo2019 . Here we present new ACS/WFC observations for PKS 0023--26 and PKS 1306--09, which were taken under HST programme GO12579 (PI J.

In the case of PKS 1549--79 the \ reference model did not allow us to recover properly the flux of some of the emission lines see also the work by Tadhunter2001,Holt2006 . Mismatching kinematics between different emission lines in the nuclear spectrum of a target is expected due to the fact that different emission lines trace gas with different levels of ionisation and/or physical conditions. In this case, we obtained an alternative reference model (that we refer to as the ` \ reference model') by fitting the 3726,29 \ doublet with the same procedure described above for the ` ' reference model.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1549--79 as a young radio-loud quasar in a gas-rich merger, where molecular gas traces accretion, a circumnuclear disc, and a compact jet-driven outflow.

quotes.quote: \ is one of the closest young, radio-loud quasars caught in an on-going merger in which the active galactic nucleus (AGN) is in the first phases of its evolution. We detect three structures tracing the accretion and the outflow of molecular gas: kpc-scale tails of gas accreting onto \ from a merger, a circumnuclear disc in the inner few hundred parsec, and a very broad ( > 2300 ) component detected in \ at the position of the AGN. Thus, in \ we see the co-existence of accretion and the ejection of gas.

\ does not seem to follow the scaling relation between bolometric luminosity and the relative importance of warm ionised and molecular outflows claimed to exist for other AGN. We argue that, although \ hosts a powerful quasar nucleus and an ultra-fast outflow, the radio jet plays a significant role in producing the outflow, which creates a cocoon of disturbed gas that expands into the circumnuclear disc. At the same time, \ has also undergone a recent major merger, as indicated by high-surface-brightness tidal tails ( Holt06,Batcheldor07 ), bringing gas into the central regions of the host galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper includes PKS 1549-79 among nearby radio galaxies with fast ionized and atomic outflows, warm turbulent H2 emission, and blue-shifted mid-infrared line wings linked to jet feedback.

quotes.quote: We present mid-IR Spitzer spectroscopy of 8 nearby radio-galaxies where fast outflows of ionized and atomic gas were detected by previous optical and radio observations. Our main results and conclusions are the following: In all of the sources, we detect high equivalent width H _2 line emission from warm (100-5000 K) molecular gas, implying warm H _2 masses ranging from 10^8 to 10^ 10 M _ . The observed H _2 to-PAH luminosity ratios are all above the values predicted from photo-ionization models, and we suggest that the H _2 emission is associated with the dissipation of a fraction of the kinetic energy provided by the radio jet.

In 5 sources (3C 236, 3C 293, 3C 459, 4C 12.50 and PKS 1549-79), we securely detect blue-shifted wings (up to 3000 km s ^ 1 ) on the [ Ne ii ] (and [ Ne iii ] for the highest SNR spectra) that matches remarkably well with the blue-shifted, broad H i absorption associated with the outflow. All but one of these sources have resolved and very broad mid-IR rotational H _2 lines with FWHM 500 km s ^ 1 (and up to 900 km s ^ 1 ), compared to only 2 overall amongst the 298 AGN with such spectra in the Spitzer archive Dasyra2011 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1549--79 as a young radio-loud quasar whose ALMA data show accretion and feedback, including a very fast, massive molecular outflow near the inner jet.

quotes.quote: The second object studied is PKS 1549--79, a quasar hosting a strong radio source in the early stages of evolution Holt06 . The ALMA CO(1-0) data show evidence for both accretion and feedback effects, shown by the presence of a fast, 2000 , outflow (Oosterloo et al. \ in prep). The molecular outflow is particularly massive, 100 , one of the largest found in radio galaxies. Although the outflow is limited to the region of the inner radio jet ( <200 pc), it is not yet clear whether the jet or the radiation is driving it. If due to the former, this would be in agreement with numerical simulations predicting that more powerful sources (like PKS 1549--79) should drive faster/more massive outflows, compared to e.g. \ what is seen in IC 5063.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1549-79 as an obscured quasar and young radio source with merger-fed gas, a circumnuclear disk, and a compact massive molecular outflow.

quotes.quote: The case of PKS 1549-69, an object hosting an obscured quasars and a young radio source, is particularly interesting. The ALMA high resolution observations show the presence of three gas structures, which can be seen in Fig. fig:pks1549 , tracing the accretion and the outflowing of molecular gas. Kiloparsec-scale tails are observed, resulting from an on-going merger and providing gas which accretes onto the centre of PKS 1549-79. At the same time, a circum-nuclear disc has formed in the inner few hundred parsec, and a very broad ( > 2300 ) component associated with fast outflowing gas is detected in CO(1-0) at the position of the AGN. As expected, the outflow is massive (up to 600 ) but, despite the fact that PKS 1549-79 should represent an ideal case of feedback in action, it is limited to the inner 200 pc.

sources.summary: The paper finds PKS 1549 79 has a compact flat-spectrum, double-sided milliarcsecond structure, different from earlier one-sided maps, and a stable jet with no measured motions.

quotes.quote: 1549 790 This compact flat-spectrum (CFS) source exhibits a classic double-sided structure at milliarcsecond resolution (see Fig. fig:wallpaper ), suggesting that its viewing angle is large. presented VLBI maps of PKS 1549 79 at 2.3 and 8.4 GHz, showing a one-sided jet structure, which was interpreted as evidence of a moderately aligned viewing angle. As discussed in , the difference between this previous map and our image could be due to the higher sensitivity of TANAMI observations. A spectral index map is shown in Fig 1549_spix .
1549 790 We find that the symmetric structure in this source is remarkably stable, with no measured jet motions, and an upper limit on the apparent speed of _ app <1.7 .

sources.summary: PKS 1549-79 is described as a radio-loud quasar observed with millimeter and VLBI data, caught early in an AGN merger with molecular gas and an AGN-driven inner outflow.

quotes.quote: (<10GeV) Quasar PKS 1549-79 was previously observed by in order to analyze its radio jet, making millimeter and very long baseline interferometry 2.3-GHz continuum observations. PKS 1549-79 is known as a radio-loud quasar, having a stronger radio emission and higher energy than the more common radio-quiet quasar ( ). PKS 1549-79 is also the closest one that has been caught merging with an AGN in the first phases of its evolution. and their team also presented CO(1-0) and CO(3-2) observations of its molecular gas. Their results found that the massive outflow of 650 M_ yr^ 1 being confined to < 120 pc of the inner galaxy suggests that the AGN is driving this outflow.

sources.summary: PKS 1549--79 is used as a best-studied young radio galaxy case with quiescent ionized halos but rapid warm outflows whose kinetic power is below feedback-model requirements.

quotes.quote: The presence of such halos is shown in Figure fig2 and Figure fig3 in the cases of PKS 1549--79 and PKS 1345+12 respectively. Thus, we have used the kinematics of the quiescent halos of ionized gas to derive the systemic velocity of the host galaxy.

From the best-studied case PKS 1549--79 (see Holt et al. 2006), we derive for the ionized gas: Mass of ionized gas in the outflow: 1.9 10^4 M_ < M_ outflow < 1.9 10^6 M_ . Mass outflow rate: 0.12 < M < 12 M_ yr ^ 1 . Energy flux: 5.1 10^ 40 erg \ s ^ 1 < E < 5.1 10^ 42 erg \ s ^ 1 . Kinetic power: 1.6 10^ 6 < E /L_ edd < 1.6 10^ 4 . From these numbers, it is clear that only a small fraction of the accretion power drives the warm outflow in PKS 1549--79 (Holt et al. 2006).

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 1549--79 as a dust-obscured powerful young radio galaxy with a massive jet-driven molecular outflow, confined to 200 pc and coexisting with a circumnuclear disk.

quotes.quote: In the dust-obscured, young and powerful radio galaxy PKS 1549--79, we detect, using ALMA, one of the most massive molecular gas outflows ( 650 ) likely driven by the radio jet in the process of clearing its way out from the enshrouding dense gas Oosterloo19 . However, the outflow is limited to the inner 200 pc of the galaxy, despite the presence of a powerful jet and also a powerful quasar AGN. A circumnuclear disc of M_ H_2 = 2.6 10^8 \ is observed and appears to co-exist with the outflow. This means that, unless the gas in the disc is replenished, on a time scale of 10^5 yr the AGN would be able to destroy the central disc of molecular gas and deplete the central region of this gas.

gmw_00004649.name: SDSS J082102.46+075145.0
gmw_00004649.overview: SDSS J082102.46+075145.0 is a nearby companion galaxy that is potentially several times more massive than the Milky Way [arXiv:1811.09653]. It is currently interacting or has previously interacted with a nearby brightest cluster galaxy, showing blue emission from recent star formation that connects to a molecular gas reservoir [arXiv:1811.09653].

sources.summary: The paper discusses SDSS J082102.46+075145.0 as a nearby galaxy interacting with the RXJ0821 BCG, assessing whether it donated or displaced the observed cold gas reservoir.

quotes.quote: The galaxy SDSS J082102.46+075145.0 is located 7.7 SE of the BCG nucleus. Optical spectroscopy indicates that its relative velocity is +77 32 with respect to the BCG BayerKim02 . Blue emission from recent star formation connects the molecular gas reservoir to the nearby galaxy. These factors suggest that the two galaxies have either interacted in the past or are currently interacting.

The nearby galaxy is potentially a few times more massive than the Milky Way. Its SDSS magnitudes, corrected for evolution and the K-correction Poggianti97 , yield an absolute i band luminosity of L_i = 1.35 10 . This includes a 25 correction for the underlying BCG flux, determined using a pair (source and background) of adjacent 1'' radius apertures in the HST F606W image.

gmw_00004650.name: BCG-S
gmw_00004650.overview: BCG-S is a southern brightest cluster galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of 0.2888 [arXiv:2311.00643]. Visual inspection and spectral decomposition indicate that the object is likely a blended superposition of two galaxies at different redshifts [arXiv:2311.00643].

sources.summary: The paper treats BCG-S as the southern brightest cluster galaxy, noting it was used as a cluster-center reference but may be a blended superposition of two galaxies.

quotes.quote: Our visual inspection of BCG-S suggests that it might be a superposition of two galaxies. Our spectral decomposition shows that indeed the BCG-S spectrum is a mixture of two galaxies: one at the cluster redshift and the other at a slightly higher redshift.
The spectroscopic redshift of BCG-S from the SDSS archive is z=0.2888 . Its color is bluer than the cluster red sequence by 0.3 (red cross in Figure fig:cmd ).

gmw_00004651.name: Source 9
gmw_00004651.overview: Source 9 is classified as a blazar [arXiv:0805.0237]. Its infrared spectrum is highly noisy and exhibits no measurable features, which is attributed to an unexpectedly low flux level [arXiv:0805.0237].

sources.summary: The paper identifies Source 9 as a blazar with much lower IRS 25-micron flux than IRAS, excludes it from luminosity plots, and notes its noisy, featureless IRS spectrum.

quotes.quote: We note that Source 9 is a blazar per78 with observed IRS flux at 25 smaller by a factor of 30 than when observed by IRAS. The source met the criteria for definition of the sample and so was observed, but this source is not included in the luminosity plots. As a result of the unexpectedly low flux, the IRS spectrum is very noisy and shows no measurable features (Figure 4). PAH limits are not meaningful for this source compared to those for other sources.

gmw_00004652.name: KCS 1166
gmw_00004652.overview: KCS 1166 is a Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy located at a redshift of z=6.518 [arXiv:0807.4634]. It exhibits a clearly asymmetric Lyman-alpha emission line and lacks a detectable continuum at shorter wavelengths [arXiv:0807.4634].

sources.summary: The paper describes KCS 1166 as a z=6.518 Lyman-emitting galaxy with asymmetric Ly-alpha emission, no short-wavelength continuum, and new near-infrared spectroscopy targeting HeII from Pop III stars.

quotes.quote: KCS 1166 is a Lyman emitting galaxy at z=6.518, discovered with slitless spectroscopy in the atmospheric window at 9100 \ ( ). In this object the Lyman line is clearly asymmetric, being steeper on the blue side, has a very large equivalent width, at least 80 \ in the rest frame, and a luminosity of 1.1 10^ 43 erg \ s^ 1 . No continuum is detected at shorter wavelengths, while it is present redward of the line. These characteristics and the fact that the HeII 1640 \ line is expected in a region of the J--band relatively free of sky emissions make KCS 1166 a very good candidate for the search of PopIII stars.

We have therefore carried out near IR spectroscopy in the J--band of KCS 1166, using SINFONI, an Integral Field Spectrograph at the VLT ( ), with a total on--source exposure time of 9 hours, equally spread over 3 consecutive nigths (Fig. fig1 ).

gmw_00004653.name: Lynx arc
gmw_00004653.overview: The Lynx arc is a lensed dwarf H II galaxy located at a redshift of z=3.357 [arXiv:1504.06948, arXiv:0807.4634]. It is magnified by a cluster environment [arXiv:1504.06948, arXiv:0807.4634] and characterized by elevated nitrogen-to-oxygen and carbon-to-oxygen abundance ratios [arXiv:2307.04234].

sources.summary: The paper presents the Lynx arc as a lensed H II galaxy whose optical and UV spectrum is modeled with starburst radiation plus shocks rather than a hidden AGN.

quotes.quote: The Lynx arc (z=3.357)is characterised by a very red R-K colour and strong, narrow emission lines. Fosbury et al claim that analysis by HST WFPC2 imaging and Keck optical and IR spectroscopy indicate a HII galaxy magnified by a factor of 10 by a cluster environment including stars with 10 ^6 K, U 1 and low metallicity (0.05 solar). In Table 6, col 11 the observed spectrum of the Lynx arc presented by Fosbury et al (2003, table 2) is reported.
