quotes.quote: Of the seven double-peaked AGN in our sample that exhibit double stellar components in AO imaging (Section multiwavelength ), we find that three (SDSS J095207.62+255257.2, SDSS J115523.74+150756.9, and SDSS J123915.40+531414.6) have double AGN emission components with spatial separations and position angles comparable to the spatial separations and position angles between the double stellar components in the corresponding AO image (Figure fig:dual_candidates ). In each case, the spatial separations are consistent to within our 1 errors and the position angles are consistent to 15^ , suggesting that the double AGN emission components are likely coincident with the double stellar components. This scenario is a signature of a late-stage galaxy merger in which the stellar remnants of the original progenitor galaxies accompany the two AGNs as they evolve into a kpc-scale pair.

Consequently, these galaxies are three of the most compelling dual AGN candidates in our sample. One of these, SDSS J095207.62+255257.2, was also identified as a dual AGN candidate by and , who both deem it a confirmed dual AGN system based on integral-field spectroscopy that shows AGN emission coincident with double stellar components in the AO imaging.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS0952+2552 as a double-nucleated merger with extended low-surface-brightness emission, a disk-plus-point-source fit, and broad central OIII outflow wings.

quotes.quote: The three objects with double-nucleated IR structure (SDSS0952+2552, SDSS1151+4711, SDSS1502+1115) show clear merger features.
SDSS0952+2552 and SDSS1151+4711 both show extended low surface brightness emission on scales 10 kpc.

Both SDSS0952+2552 and SDSS1151+4711 are best fit by a disk component plus point source, though with considerable residuals due to the disturbed nature. A multi-component fit is required for SDSS1502+1115 as well as SDSS1715+6008, both are best fit by a point source as well as both a disk and bulge component. Details of the host galaxy fits are provided in Table T:hosts . An interesting finding of our deep imaging study is that out of the four objects studied, all but SDSS1715+6008 have close faint companions. In the case of SDSS0952+2552 and SDSS1151+4711 the companions are faint disk galaxies with separations of 50 kpc, connected to the main galaxy through extended emission. In the case of SDSS1502+1115, the separation is somewhat larger ( 100 kpc) and the companion is unresolved. The slit covers the companions for all three objects, and for two objects either absorption or emission at the redshift of the main object is detected. This will be discussed in more detail in Section S:spec . In summary, all but one of the objects studied show clear signs of interaction with strong tidal tails and disturbed morphologies, consistent with ongoing mergers. The remaining object, which is an X-ray binary AGN with considerably smaller separation than the double-nuclei AGN ( 2 kpc, compared to 5-8 kpc for the double-nuclei sources) shows an undisturbed morphology even at the low surface brightnesses reached. The smaller separation in this source suggests a later merger stage, broadly consistent with the less disturbed morphology. The 2D fits in u//z provide integrated and spatially resolved information on the host galaxy colors. The u- colors place the galaxies in the red sequence e.g. strateva_color_2001,balogh_bimodal_2004,schawinski_galaxy_2010 , consistent with quenched star formation. However, dust reddening can cause similarly red u- colors e.g. cardamone_dust-corrected_2010 . Spatially resolved colors are shown in Figure F:colourmaps . SDSS0952+2552, SDSS1151+4711 and SDSS1502+1115 all show redder z colors towards the central peaks, the u band emission appears to be centred on the areas with redder z colors. The redder colors can be interpreted both as stronger reddening towards the central region as well as strong H emission since H falls in the z band at the redshifts of these three objects. Combined with the co-spatial u band emission tracing younger stellar populations, the color maps are consistent with central compact ( < 5 kpc) starbursts. Note that in the case of SDSS1151+4711 and SDSS1502+1115, due to the disturbed morphology, artefacts from the PSF fit were visible in the most central pixels. Additionally, the extreme reddening seen in the very center of SDSS1151+4711 might be an indication that the point source has been slightly over-subtracted due to the complex nature of the profile fit of the merging galaxies. SDSS1715+6008 shows a color structure quite different from the other sources. The u band emission is extended and without clear structure while the z color map shows a single funnel-shaped area of almost one magnitude redder emission in the south-west corner of the galaxy. Due to the lower redshift OIII falls just in the blue edge of the band for SDSS1715+6008. The reddened wedge could therefore either be interpreted as an area with higher extinction or a region where OIII emission is suppressed. Unfortunately, the slit placement was almost exactly orthogonal to the wedge feature and therefore the strength of OIII in the reddened area cannot be assessed. Spectroscopy: Kinematics Long-slit spectra provide detailed spatially resolved information about gas and stellar kinematics. In all plots and analysis we use redshifts from smith_search_2010 . When available, we use redshifts determined from stellar absorption lines, for the two cases where no host galaxy redshift could be determined (SDSS1151+4711, SDSS1502+1115), we use the redshift of the OIII emission, in particular, the higher redshift OIII line is used. Figures F:twod_0952 F:twod_1715 show the 2D spectra for our AGN sample covering H and the OIII 5007/4969 doublet. Additionally, detailed line fits are performed, as described in Section S:obs . All details of the fits are summarized in the Appendix and in Tables T:lines_0952 T:lines_1715b as well as Figures F:lines_0952 , F:lines_1151 , F:lines_1502 , F:lines_1715 (detailed maps of individual lines in each spatial slice) and Figures F:spec_0952 , F:spec_1151 , F:spec_1502 , F:spec_1715 (profiles of OIII doublet and H in different spatial slices). From these fits, we derive the kinematics of the gas and stars along the slit (see Fig. F:kinematics ). Our spatial resolution corresponds to 1-5 kpc, depending on object. Clearly extended OIII emission, out to scales of 50-100 kpc is observed in all objects in the sample (see Fig. F:twod_0952 F:twod_1715 ). Clear clumps in the NLR are apparent in the three sources with NIR double nuclei (see Fig. F:twod_0952 F:twod_1715 ). In two cases, the clumps are unresolved at the spatial resolution of our data ( 1-5 kpc, Fig. F:twod_1151 ; SDSS1151+4711 and Fig. F:twod_1502 ; SDSS1502+1115). These NLR sizes are consistent with those of AGN with similar OIII luminosities bennert_size_2002,schmitt_hubble_2003,bennert_size_2006,fu_extended_2009,humphrey_integral-field_2010,greene_feedback_2011,liu_observations_2013 , while the overall NLR sizes are comparable to AGN two orders of magnitude brighter liu_observations_2013 . The spatial and kinematic structure of the OIII features in our sample differ quite significantly between sources. Amongst all sources, a range of kinematic structures is observed, including very spatially extended OIII emission with narrow line width and no velocity gradients (seen in SDSS0952+2552; see Fig. F:twod_0952 , F:lines_0952 , F:spec_0952 , and SDSS1502+1115; see Fig. F:twod_0952 , F:lines_0952 , F:spec_0952 ), compact OIII emission that is spatially un- or marginally resolved (seen in SDSS1151+4711; Fig. F:twod_1151 , and SDSS1502+1115; Fig. F:twod_1502 ) as well as extended OIII emission with substantial line widths and gradients (seen in SDSS0952+2552; Fig. F:twod_0952 , SDSS1502+1115; Fig. F:twod_1502 , and SDSS1715+6008; Fig. F:twod_1715 ). Many of the objects show several distinct features, indicating a NLR consisting of spatially separated components. In SDSS0952+2552, SDSS1151+4711 and SDSS1502+1115 broad wings in OIII with velocities up to 1500 km s ^ 1 are detected (Figures F:spec_0952 , F:spec_1151 , F:spec_1502 ). In SDSS0952+2552 (Fig. F:twod_0952 ), two OIII clouds extend from the center, the southern (Slice A) part shows red-shifted OIII emission, while the northern (Slice E) part shows narrow slightly blue-shifted emission tracing along the bridge that connects the source with its nearby companion. Both features have line widths 200 km s ^ 1 , consistent with significant internal broadening. The extent of this blue-shifted feature is quite remarkable since it spans about 50 kpc with little or no velocity gradient. OIII is also detected in the neighbouring galaxy. The two components are kinematically separated by 500 km s ^ 1 (Fig. F:kinematics ). The primary Type 1 AGN is at the same velocity as the blue-shifted feature, while the suspected secondary is associated with the red-shifted component. While the two components are not spatially separated, they appear to be physically separate features given the abrupt change in velocity, there is no smooth transition in velocity between the two features, but rather an overlap, indicating that the NLR consists of spatially distinct clumps. At the location of the continuum source, OIII shows strong broad wings out to >1000 km s ^ 1 with respect to systemic, indicative of an outflow.

sources.summary: SDSS J0952+2552 is treated as a major-merger dual/offset AGN candidate whose two emission components match two stellar bulges; the NE source is Type 1 and the SW source Type 2.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0952+2552 was selected as a Type 1 double-peaked AGN in , and follow-up observations revealed two emission components separated by 1 00 (4.85 kpc; MC11.1,CO12.1,FU12.1 ) that spatially coincide with two stellar bulges seen in high resolution near-infrared imaging RO11.1,FU12.1 . We also observe these stellar bulges in our HST /F160W data. Based on extracted spectra for each individual source, concluded that J0952+2552NE is a Type 1 AGN while J0952+2552SW is a Type 2 AGN. We make 0.3-8 keV X-ray detections of J0952+2552NE at >5 and J0952+2552SW at 0.5 , and we find that the X-ray source positions agree with those of the stellar bulges (observed with HST /F160W) to within 1.2 times the astrometric error. The 2.0:1 merger ratio indicates an ongoing major merger.

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSS J0952+2552 as a candidate dual AGN system with both galaxies inside the SDSS fiber, outflow kinematics, and two stellar bulges seen in HST, NIRC2, and OSIRIS imaging.

quotes.quote: Of these eight dual AGN candidates, four (SDSS J0952+2552, SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1248 0257, and SDSS J1301 0058) have a companion galaxy located within the 1 5 radius SDSS fiber. These systems are kinematically classified as outflow (SDSS J0952+2552) or ambiguous (SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1248 0257, and SDSS J1301 0058). However, because both galaxies are located within the SDSS fiber, the motion of dual AGNs could contribute to the double-peaked emission lines in these systems.

SDSS J0952+2552 (candidate dual AGNs). The two stellar bulges seen in the HST /F160W image are also seen in near-infrared laser guide star adaptive optics H band imaging with the NIRC2 near-infrared camera on the Keck II telescope RO11.1 and in H band imaging with the OH-Suppressing Infrared Imaging Spectrograph (OSIRIS) on the Keck II telescope FU12.1 . While previous studies have classified this system as confirmed dual AGNs MC11.1,FU12.1,MC15.1 , we classify it as candidate dual AGNs because of the need for X-ray or radio confirmation of two AGNs with such a small separation ( 1 00 ).

gmw_00004689.name: SDSS J123915.40+531414.6
gmw_00004689.overview: SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 is a complex active galactic nucleus candidate with four distinct emission components [arXiv:1504.01391, arXiv:1111.2862] and two stellar bulges [arXiv:1504.01391]. The system exhibits double-peaked emission lines [arXiv:1504.01391, arXiv:2306.08376] and may host a triple active galactic nucleus or a dual active galactic nucleus with outflows [arXiv:1111.2862].

sources.summary: SDSS J1239+5314 is a complex dual/offset AGN candidate with four emission components, two stellar bulges, and X-ray sources aligned with the candidate AGNs rather than the main double peaks.

quotes.quote: This system was identified as a Type 1 double-peaked AGN in , and follow-up long-slit observations revealed that this system is much more complex than its SDSS spectrum alone suggests. The long-slit spectra show four emission components that are distinct both spatially and in line-of-sight velocity CO12.1 . The double-peaked profile of the SDSS spectrum corresponds to two emission components separated by only 0 39 (1.3 kpc), and our HST F160W observations show a stellar bulge coincident with the blueshifted emission component (J1239+5314NE) but do not show a stellar bulge coincident with the redshifted emission component. Instead, there is a third emission component (which has a redshift between the blueshifted and redshifted components of the main double peak) at a larger distance ( 1 20 , or 3.98 kpc; also reported in FU12.1 ) that does coincide with a stellar bulge (J1239+5314SW), seen both in K_p imaging FU12.1 and in our HST /F160W imaging (Figure fig:images ). This third emission component would be contained within the 3^ diameter SDSS fiber, so that the resultant SDSS spectrum contains contributions from three spatially-distinct emission components.

We detect two 0.3-8 keV X-ray sources, at >5 and 1.0 significances, that have a similar spatial separation and orientation on the sky as the two emission components, to within 3 . Since the X-ray spectrum is not soft and each source has a high soft X-ray to luminosity ratio (Table tbl-3 ; Table tbl-4 ), we conclude that photoionization is not likely producing these sources. While one X-ray source spatially coincides ( 0.16 difference in spatial position) with the less massive stellar bulge, J1239+5314NE, the other X-ray source is located 3.57 away from the more massive stellar bulge, J1239+5314SW.

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 as a QSO-catalog source whose spectra and diffuse radio lobes make its quasar label doubtful and suggest a short transitional phase.

quotes.quote: Only two of them -- SDSS J082905.01+571541.6 and SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 - were classified as QSOs and as such listed in the quasar catalogue based on Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) Data Release 7 DR7QSO . Some of their physical data taken from Shen2010 are quoted in Table qso . Their NVSS and FIRST radio maps along with SDSS spectra are shown in Figs. 082904+571541 and 123915+531416 , respectively. Although broad and narrow emission lines typical for QSOs are present there, the continua, unlike in QSOs, do not rise towards the blue end of the spectra. This makes labelling these two objects as quasars questionable. Instead, they should be named radio galaxies, but the diffuse nature of their radio lobes casts doubt on this classification as well.

Two objects in our sample -- SDSS J082905.01+571541.6 and SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 shown in Figs. 082904+571541 and 123915+531416 , respectively -- are also rare and accordingly likely to remain in a short, transitional phase.

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 as an unusual, compelling candidate whose emission components may indicate a triple AGN or dual AGN with outflows.

quotes.quote: We note that SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 is an unusual case because the two emission features corresponding to the double peaks in the SDSS spectrum have a significantly smaller spatial offset (1.3 h^ 1 _ 70 kpc, or 0 39) than the spatial offset between the two stellar components visible in the AO image (4.1 h^ 1 _ 70 kpc, or 1 24; FU11.1 ). Instead, we find a third emission component (labeled B in Figure fig:dual_candidates , bottom) between the blueward (labeled A) and redward (labeled C) features corresponding to the double peaks. This third emission component has a spatial offset ( 4.17 0.72 h^ 1 _ 70 kpc, or 1 20 0 21 ) and position angle ( 32^ .5 3^ .3 ) relative to the blueward component that is consistent with those of the double stellar components.

Note that there is also a fourth emission component (labeled D), visible only in the two-dimensional spectrum taken at position angle =124^ .4 , that is 5^ from the target galaxy's stellar continuum and may be reflective of an outflow. The long-slit and AO observations of SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 suggest it might host a triple AGN system or a dual AGN system with outflows.

sources.summary: The paper flags SDSS J123915.40+531414.6 as a dual-quasar candidate with a distinct double-peaked emission line, Gaussian-fitted components, and unresolved Pan-STARRS structure.

quotes.quote: There is one target, SDSS J123915.40+531414.6, with a distinct double-peaked emission line feature which has already been reported by Smith2010 . We use one Gaussian and two Gaussians to fit the broad and narrow components, respectively. The best fitting results are shown in the top left panel of Figure fig:f4 . We further make optical confirmations via the Pan-STARRS composite colour image with the Gaia detections superimposed, which has not been considered before. However, due to the limited resolution of the Pan-STARRS image, the structure of this target is still elusive.

gmw_00004690.name: PKS 1351 018
gmw_00004690.overview: PKS 1351 018 is a luminous, radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of approximately 3.7 [arXiv:2107.00716, arXiv:1202.2193]. It exhibits blazar-like behavior, featuring a compact radio core, a jet that bends by 130 degrees, and strong gamma-ray flares [arXiv:2107.00716, arXiv:1202.2193].

sources.summary: The paper presents PKS 1351 018 as a luminous high-redshift blazar/quasar with steady synchrotron emission, gamma-ray flares, a compact radio source, and a young powerful jet.

quotes.quote: PKS 1351 018 at a redshift of z=3.71 is one of the most luminous, steady synchrotron sources with a luminosity > 10^ 47 erg s ^ 1 . The synchrotron luminosity does not seem to vary by more than 25 over 35 years.

In spite of this steady behavior, two strong ray flares 10^ 49 erg s ^ 1 were detected in 2011 and 2016. There is blazar-like behavior coexisting with the steady behavior. This study is aimed at elucidating the dual nature of this source.

We find that the radio source is extremely compact with a bright core and a steep spectrum secondary component, 12 mas away, that appears to be constant in position and flux density in six epochs from 1995 to 2018. We estimate that a jet with a time averaged power of (5.2 3.2) 10^ 45 erg s ^ 1 terminates in this lobe that is advancing 0.9 at a deprojected distance of 1-3 kpc from the central engine. This is the rare case of a young ( 6000 yr), very powerful radio source that is viewed a few degrees from the jet axis.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1351-018 as a z=3.707 radio-loud quasar with a complex pc-scale jet bending by 130 degrees, where polarization reveals an inner jet component near the core.

quotes.quote: 1351-018 This radio loud quasar is the third most distant source in our sample with a redshift of 3.707 osmer1994 . It was observed by the VSOP Space-VLBI project at 1.6 and 5 GHz frey2002 . It has a complex pc-scale structure with the jet appearing to bend through 130^ . The high resolution space-VLBI images ( 1.56 0.59 mas at 5 GHz) clearly resolve an inner jet component within 1 mas of the core with a position angle of 120^ , which was also detected at 8.6 GHz by fey2000 . Our 5-GHz image is dominated by the core emission, but the polarization gives a clear indication of the presence of an inner jet component, which is also indicated by the intensity model fitting.

gmw_00004691.name: TDG candidate
gmw_00004691.overview: This tidal dwarf galaxy candidate is located within the interaction debris of its parent galaxies [arXiv:1712.04913]. It features an elongated structure with two principal star-forming knots and lacks indicators of an old stellar population [arXiv:1712.04913].

sources.summary: The paper validates the Arp 202 TDG candidate through H-alpha imaging, velocity, oxygen abundance, weak old-population indicators, and its location in interaction debris.

quotes.quote: Our \ imaging reveals the TDG to have an elongated structure, 1.9 kpc in length with the two principal star forming knots at either end. Our observations also show the TDG candidate has a recessional V _ H 3032 , within 100 \ of the parent pair's mean velocity and an oxygen abundance of 12+log(O/H) = 8.10 0.41. The TDG's oxygen abundance is in good agreement with that of a star forming region in NGC 2719A, one of the parent galaxies, which has an estimated oxygen abundance of 12+log(O/H) = 8.05 0.41. The TDG's V _ H and oxygen abundance confirm previous results validating the candidate as a TDG.

The absence of detectable emission from the TDG in Spitzer 3.6 m and 4.5 m images together with the lack of absorption lines and weak continuum in the spectrum is consistent with absence of an old population ( 0.5 Gyr). The location of the TDG within the interaction debris and the absence of indicators of an old stellar population in the TDG are consistent with a scenario in which the TDG is formed from \ stripped from the parent galaxies and within the extended dark matter halo of one of the parents as proposed by bournaud03,duc04 .

gmw_00004692.name: Arp 202 interacting galaxy pair
gmw_00004692.overview: Arp 202 is an interacting galaxy pair consisting of NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A [arXiv:1712.04913, arXiv:1407.7643]. An HI tidal tail emanates from NGC 2719A and connects the parent galaxies to a star-forming tidal dwarf galaxy candidate [arXiv:1712.04913, arXiv:1407.7643].

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp 202 as an interacting pair whose tidal debris links NGC 2719A, the parent galaxies, and the TDG candidate in gas and star formation.

quotes.quote: In this paper we present \ imaging from the Himalayan Chandra Telescope https://www.iiap.res/iao_telescope (HCT) of the black Arp 202 interacting galaxy pair (NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A) and their TDG candidate (Figure fig1 ), together with a black Gemini Multi--Object Spectrograph (GMOS) optical spectrum for the TDG candidate. Previous UV ( GALEX ) observations revealed a very blue (FUV - g = -- 0.47) diffuse object at the end black of a tidal tail eminating from NGC 2719A smith10a , which the authors classified as a TDG candidate. However, \ imaging with the SARA Southeastern Association for Research in Astronomy 0.9-m telescope at Kitt Peak smith10b and the Spitzer at 8 m imaging, reported in , failed to black detect counterparts for the TDG black candidate.

Our GMRT \ mapping of Arp 202 seng14 revealed an \ tidal tail, which emanates from NGC 2719A and extends to the projected position of the TDG candidate. The \ mapping provided black morphological and kinematic evidence linking the interacting pair, the \ tidal tail and the \ TDG counterpart.

The average of the black optical radial velocities of NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A is 3097 . Using this average velocity and assuming H _0 = 75 \ Mpc ^ −1 , we adopt a distance of 41.3 Mpc to NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A and the TDG. At this distance the spatial scale is 12 kpc black arcmin ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper strengthens the tidal-dwarf interpretation for the Arp 202 candidate through the tidal-tail connection, dynamical-mass estimate, and lack of clear dark-matter evidence.

quotes.quote: The candidate TDG of the Arp 202 system first reported the presence of a clumpy star forming tidal tail terminating in black the TDG candidate from NUV ( GALEX ) observations and confirmed the candidate was part of the Arp 202 system with optical spectroscopy. Our GMRT \ morphology and kinematic results, set out in section results , clearly show that the \ tidal tail/bridge links the \ TDG counterpart to the interacting Arp 202 pair.

black For the Arp 202 TDG candidate, M _ dyn is estimated at 3.9 10 ^8 \ using the method described in Table table3 . This is 4 times the M _ HI + M* with a ratio of the same order black as in NGC 4656UV. We have no M _ dyn for Holmberg IX or Arp 305. As for the Arp 202 TDG candidate, black we see no clear indication of the presence of significant amounts of dark matter.

sources.summary: The paper describes Arp 202 as a pre-merger pair whose H i morphology, tidal tail, and candidate tidal dwarf are linked to the interaction between its two galaxies.

quotes.quote: Received ; accepted firstpage - lastpage We present results from our Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope (GMRT) \ observations of the interacting pair Arp 202 ( black NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A ). black Earlier deep UV (GALEX) observations of this system revealed a tidal tail like extension with a diffuse object towards its end, proposed as a tidal dwarf galaxy (TDG) candidate. We detect H i emission from the Arp 202 system black , including \ counterparts for the tidal tail and the TDG candidate. Our GMRT \ morphological and kinematic results clearly link the \ tidal tail and the \ TDG counterparts to the interaction between NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A, thus strengthening the case for the TDG.

gmw_00004693.name: NGC 2719A
gmw_00004693.overview: NGC 2719A is an irregular galaxy [arXiv:1407.7643] that is undergoing a close interaction with its companion, NGC 2719 [arXiv:1407.7643]. This interaction has produced a tidal tail originating from NGC 2719A [arXiv:1407.7643, arXiv:1712.04913] and has triggered star formation in several knots across its optical disk [arXiv:1712.04913].

sources.summary: NGC 2719A is presented as the irregular Arp 202 component from which the tidal tail likely originates, having lost much of its H i and stellar material to the interaction.

quotes.quote: The abrupt change of rotation pattern in the contours together with the tidal tail morphology at velocities below 3050 \ indicates the \ there has been strongly perturbed by the interaction between NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A. The narrow range of \ velocities, 60 , in the tidal tail running westward from NGC 2719A to and including the TDG candidate provide clear evidence linking the both the tidal tail and TDG candidate to the interaction between NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A. Arp 202 -- major interactions The pre-merger interacting pair black , NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A, have an optical radial velocity separation of 40 \ and D _ 25 of 1.3 arcsec (17.5 kpc) and 0.9 arcsec (12 kpc) respectively. We estimate the stellar mass for NGC 2719 at 3.4 x 10 ^9 \ and NGC 2719A at 5.0 x 10 ^9 \ (a stellar mass ratio of 1:1.4), based on the parameters from and for SDSS g -band magnitudes and g-- colours.

NGC 2719A has a more irregular optical morphology, and the UV tail (Figure fig2 ) appears to originate from this galaxy. It seems likely the \ and stellar material in black the tidal tail has its origin in a close interaction between NGC 2719 and NGC 2719A, where NGC 2719A has lost black most of its \ to NGC 2719 and the IGM.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2719A as a parent galaxy of the Arp 202 TDG, the source of a tidal tail, and a host of H-alpha star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: Previous UV ( GALEX ) observations revealed a very blue (FUV - g = -- 0.47) diffuse object at the end black of a tidal tail eminating from NGC 2719A smith10a , which the authors classified as a TDG candidate.
Our GMRT \ mapping of Arp 202 seng14 revealed an \ tidal tail, which emanates from NGC 2719A and extends to the projected position of the TDG candidate.

The SF regions, numbered 1, 2 and 3, in the image are projected against black or near the edge--on optical disk of NGC 2719, while knots 4, 5 and 6 are projected against black or near the optical disk of NGC 2719A. An area of low density \ emission between knots 3 and 4 may be part of a tidal bridge between the principal pair. Figure fig2 (a) also shows an extensive area of low surface brightness emission N of the NGC 2719 optical centre.

gmw_00004694.name: VV304a
gmw_00004694.overview: VV304a is a low-inclination, late-type spiral galaxy with physical properties similar to the Milky Way [arXiv:2011.12323]. Its two-dimensional line-of-sight velocity field reveals a global, extended perturbation likely driven by a recent interaction with its massive companion, VV304b [arXiv:2011.12323].

sources.summary: The paper presents VV304a as a Milky-Way-type late spiral whose line-of-sight velocity field shows interaction-driven vertical corrugation rather than bar or spiral streaming.

quotes.quote: We study the two dimensional (2D) line-of-sight velocity ( V_ los ) field of the low-inclination, late-type galaxy VV304a. The resulting 2D kinematic map reveals a global, coherent and extended perturbation that is likely associated with a recent interaction with the massive companion VV304b. We use multi-band imaging and a suite of test particle simulations to quantify the plausible strength of in-plane flows due to non-axisymmetric perturbations and show that the observed velocity flows are much too large to be driven either by spiral structure nor by a bar.

VV304a is located at a distance of 54 Mpc, has an optical radius of R_ opt 18 kpc and a rotational velocity curve that peaks at 245 km s ^ 1 (TF14). Based on the observed circular velocity profile, obtained using H_ Fabry-Perot observations (see Sec sec:2.2 ), TF14 estimated a total VV304a dynamical mass within R_ opt of M (<R_ opt ) 2.5 10^ 11 M _ . These broad properties are similar to those of the Milky Way 2017MNRAS.465...76M, 2017RAA....17...96L, 2020MNRAS.494.4291C .

gmw_00004695.name: VV304b
gmw_00004695.overview: VV304b is a late-type companion galaxy that has recently interacted with the Milky Way-type galaxy VV304a [arXiv:2011.12323]. It features a clear double-barred structure and well-defined grand design spiral arms [arXiv:2011.12323].

sources.summary: The paper treats VV304b as VV304a's massive late-type companion, linking it to the recent interaction and noting its double-barred morphology and spiral arms.

quotes.quote: Here, we present a full 2D kinematic map of a Milky Way-type galaxy, namely VV304a, that shows a global, coherent and extended perturbation consistent with a corrugation pattern, likely associated with the recent interaction with its massive companion, VV304b.

VV304a: General properties VV304a is a late-type galaxy that has recently interacted with its closest companion, VV304b hereafter TF14 2014MNRAS.442.2188T . VV304a is located at a distance of 54 Mpc, has an optical radius of R_ opt 18 kpc and a rotational velocity curve that peaks at 245 km s ^ 1 (TF14). Based on the observed circular velocity profile, obtained using H_ Fabry-Perot observations (see Sec sec:2.2 ), TF14 estimated a total VV304a dynamical mass within R_ opt of M (<R_ opt ) 2.5 10^ 11 M _ . These broad properties are similar to those of the Milky Way 2017MNRAS.465...76M, 2017RAA....17...96L, 2020MNRAS.494.4291C . Its companion, VV304b, also a late-type galaxy, has an optical radius of 18 kpc and a peak circular rotational velocity of 200 km s ^ 1 .

Visual inspection of this panel reveals that, unlike VV304b which shows a clear double barred structure and well defined grand design spiral arms, VV304a does not possess any clear strong m=2 perturbation.

gmw_00004696.name: B2 1215+30
gmw_00004696.overview: B2 1215+30 is a BL Lac-type blazar active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1701.01067]. It has exhibited a large-amplitude TeV flare with rapid variability and a correlated high GeV flux state [arXiv:1701.01067].

sources.summary: The paper reports a luminous TeV flare from the BL Lac blazar B2 1215+30, with rapid variability, correlated GeV emission, no optical counterpart, and strong Doppler beaming.

quotes.quote: 2 is a BL Lac-type blazar that was first detected at TeV energies by the MAGIC atmospheric Cherenkov telescopes, and subsequently confirmed by the VERITAS observatory with data collected between 2009 and 2012. In 2014 February 08, VERITAS detected a large-amplitude flare from 2 during routine monitoring observations of the blazar 1ES 1218+304, located in the same field of view. The TeV flux reached 2.4 times the Crab Nebula flux with a variability timescale of <3.6 h. Multiwavelength observations with Fermi LAT, Swift , and the Tuorla observatory revealed a correlated high GeV flux state and no significant optical counterpart to the flare, with a spectral energy distribution where the gamma-ray luminosity exceeds the synchrotron luminosity. When interpreted in the framework of a one-zone leptonic model, the observed emission implies a high degree of beaming, with Doppler factor > 10 , and an electron population with spectral index p<2.3 .

2 (R.A. = 12^ h 17^ m 52^ s , decl. = +30^ 07^ 00 ^ 1 , J2000), also known as ON 325 or 1ES 1215+303, was first detected at TeV energies by MAGIC magic_1215 . At GeV energies it is associated with 3FGL J1217.8+3007 3fgl . There is some uncertainty in the distance to this source, with values of z = 0.130 akiyama03 and z=0.237 lanzetta93 being quoted for its spectroscopic redshift.

gmw_00004697.name: CTA 102
gmw_00004697.overview: CTA 102 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar that experienced an extended period of activity from 2016 to 2017 [arXiv:1911.12087]. During this flaring state, several strong gamma-ray flares were observed, showing a spectral cutoff that is likely caused by an intrinsic break in the energy distribution of emitting particles within its jet [arXiv:1911.12087].

sources.summary: The paper studies CTA 102 during its 2016-2017 flaring state, finding spectral cutoffs, modeling multiwavelength emission, and attributing the break to the emitting particle distribution.

quotes.quote: The flat spectrum radio quasar CTA 102 entered an extended period of activity from 2016 to 2017 during which several strong ray flares were observed. Using Fermi large area telescope data a detailed investigation of spectra of CTA 102 during the flaring period is performed. In several periods the spectrum is not consistent with a simple power-law, having a hard photon index with an index of (1.8-2.0) that shows a spectral cutoff around an observed photon energy of (9-16) GeV. The internal ray absorption via photon-photon pair production on the broad-line-region-reflected photons cannot account for the observed cut-off/break even if the emitting region is very close to the central source. This cut-off/break is likely due to a similar intrinsic break in the energy distribution of emitting particles.

The origin of the spectral break is investigated through the multiwavelength modeling of the spectral energy distribution, considering a different location for the emitting region. The observed X-ray and ray data is modeled as inverse Compton scattering of synchrotron and/or external photons on the electron population that produce the radio-to-optical emission which allowed to constrain the power-law index and cut-off energy in the electron energy distribution. The obtained results are discussed in the context of a diffusive acceleration of electrons in the CTA 102 jet.

gmw_00004698.name: NGC 1187
gmw_00004698.overview: NGC 1187 is classified as an SBc barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:0806.0471]. The galaxy features a central bar structure from which three spiral arms emerge [arXiv:0806.0471].

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 1187 as a barred SBc galaxy with three observed arms and notes that a fast-bar solution remains possible.

quotes.quote: NGC 1187 : this SBc ( T=5 ) galaxy has a bar with three spiral arms emerging from it. The best-fitting model (see Fig. modsel1 ), setting R 2 , has four arms due to using even Fourier components, three of which corresponds well the observed arms. The error estimate for R is quite large, and a fast-bar solution cannot be ruled out.

gmw_00004699.name: NGC 1832
gmw_00004699.overview: NGC 1832 is a barred spiral galaxy characterized by an asymmetric spiral structure and a large inner ring [arXiv:0806.0471]. The galaxy hosts resolved star-forming knots, gas-emission knots at larger radii, and a negative oxygen-abundance gradient across its bar region [arXiv:1204.6212].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 1832 as a barred galaxy with a large inner ring, asymmetric spiral structure, and a model reproducing the ring and one arm.

quotes.quote: NGC 1832 : this SBbc ( T=4 ) galaxy has an inner ring whose major axis seems to be larger than the bar. The spiral structure asymmetric. The ring and one spiral arm are reproduced by the best-fitting model (see Fig. modsel1 ), setting R 1.7 .

sources.summary: The paper resolves star-forming knots in NGC 1832, finds larger-radius gas-emission knots, and reports a negative oxygen-abundance gradient across bar emission.

quotes.quote: In NGC 1832 and NGC 1530 we resolve knots of star formation (SF hereafter) at radius R 0.25 R _ 25 ( R_ 25 being the length of the projected major axis of a galaxy at the isophotal level 25 mag/arcsec ^2 in the B-band).

For seven galaxies (NGC 1433, NGC 1530, NGC 1832, NGC 2665, NGC 2935, NGC 3081 and NGC 4314) we have also found knots of gas emission at larger radii, corresponding to one or several crowded \ regions.

If we consider all the detected emission in the bar region at a 3- significance, for the star formation galaxies, we detect a positive gradient for three galaxies (NGC 1169, NGC 2523 and NGC 2935) and a negative gradient is observed for two galaxies (NGC 1832 and NGC 2962).

gmw_00004700.name: NGC 3275
gmw_00004700.overview: NGC 3275 is classified as a barred spiral galaxy of type SBab [arXiv:0806.0471]. The galaxy's structure features an inner ring, an asymmetric spiral structure, and a lopsided inner ring that lies within its bar radius [arXiv:0806.0471].

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 3275 as a barred galaxy with an inner ring and asymmetric spiral structure, while noting Fourier phase overestimates the bar radius.

quotes.quote: NGC 3275 : this is a SBab ( T=2 ) galaxy with an inner ring and asymmetric spiral structure. The ring and part of the spiral structure is reproduced with R 1.5 (see Fig. modsel1 ). The Fourier phase angle method clearly overestimates the bar radius: it is outside the lopsided inner ring.

gmw_00004701.name: NGC 3507
gmw_00004701.overview: NGC 3507 is a barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0107100, arXiv:0806.0471] located 16.3 Mpc away [arXiv:1612.06794] that serves as the brightest member of a small galaxy group [arXiv:astro-ph/0107100]. It features two long, symmetric spiral arms [arXiv:astro-ph/0107100, arXiv:0806.0471] and exhibits a low star formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0107100].

sources.summary: The paper questions the AGN classification of NGC 3507, finding its X-ray continuum poorly described by a power law and its nuclear radio emission possibly contaminated.

quotes.quote: NGC 3507 is located at a distance of 16.3 Mpc. We extracted the spectrum from the central core and modeled it using an absorbed power law. The resulting fit is unacceptable with a reduced ^2>3 .

It appears that the X-ray continuum is not well described by a power law. This issue is examined further in the discussion section. The 0.5-10 keV unabsorbed luminosity of the central region (based on the power law component for the above fit) is 3.53 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 (total source luminosity including a diffuse, extra-nuclear component 1.07 10^ 39 erg s ^ 1 ). We detect a radio counterpart to the optical nucleus of NGC 3507 in both sub-bands. Gaussian fitting in the image plane indicates that the source is marginally resolved.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3507 as a barred spiral group member with regular arms, low inferred star formation, and 90 detected young star groupings concentrated along inner arms.

quotes.quote: NGC 3507 is a typical barred galaxy (SBb type), with two long regular symmetric arms circling around the nucleus and the bar. The inner parts of each arm are more regular than the outer parts. The isophotal major axis is D_ 25 =3.4 (RC3), the inclination is 26 ^ (Schulman et al. 1997) while the position angle is about 90 ^ (de Jong van der Kruit 1994). According to Garcia et al. (1993), NGC 3507 is the brightest member of a small group of galaxies.

This paper concludes that in NGC 3507 there are no High Velocity Clouds (HVCs, i.e. HI regions with peculiar motion). Since HVCs are thought to be star formation indicators (being probably originated by Supernova explosions) the lack of HVCs in NGC 3507 suggests a low star formation rate in this galaxy. Fig. 2 shows the map of the 90 YSGs detected: most of the objects are located along the inner part of the arms.

sources.summary: The atlas classifies NGC 3507 as Disky, matching large-scale H-alpha and CO emission along dust lanes, and notes CO kinematics could test a putative outflow.

quotes.quote: NGC 3507 (see Fig. Figure_AB2 ): We have classified the ionised gas morphology of this galaxy as Disky . Previous H imaging from the literature SanchezGallego2012 trace the large scale ionised gas distribution that is also seen in our images, but with worse seeing (theirs 1.3 \ vs ours 1.0 ). The CO map from the PHANGS-ALMA survey, which provides the CO(2-1) line emission at about 1 \ resolution Leroy2021 , traces molecular gas along the dust-lanes seen in the sharp-divided images, broadly resembling the ionised gas emission. A detailed analysis of the CO kinematics would reveal whether the atomic gas is participating from the putative outflow indicated by the H morphology.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3507 as having central diffuse X-ray emission and an extended radio source, concluding its LINER emission is likely powered by stellar processes rather than an AGN.

quotes.quote: NGC 3507 (L2, SBb) has two point sources in its central kiloparsec: one very faint (14 counts) close to the edge of the region and a central extended source embedded in a bright patch of diffuse emission. The central source had only 187 counts, which did not allow us to test a wide variety of spectral models. Instead we estimated its luminosity by fitting a simple mekal model (the photon index of the best-fitting power-law model was unrealistic) and we find L_ 0.5-10 keV =1.2 10^ 39 erg s ^ 1 after correcting for absorption. A weak, extended radio source ( S_ =16 mJy) is detected at 1.45 GHz C87 , which suggests that this object is not a LLAGN.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 3507 has very long spiral arms and one of the best-fitting models, reproducing the full spiral structure with R 1.2.

quotes.quote: NGC 3507 : this SBb ( T=3 ) galaxy has really long spiral arms that can be followed about 360 ^ . The best-fitting model, setting R 1.2 , reproduces the spiral structure in its full extent. Overall, this is one of the best models (see Fig. modsel1 ).

gmw_00004702.name: LAB
gmw_00004702.overview: LAB is a highly luminous Lyman-alpha blob located at a redshift of z = 2.83 [arXiv:0806.4384]. The galaxy is extended over at least 95 kpc and is likely ionized by cold accretion rather than an active galactic nucleus or an energetic starburst [arXiv:0806.4384].

sources.summary: The paper studies a z=2.83 Lyman-alpha blob counterpart, finding weak AGN and starburst evidence, favoring cold accretion ionization and a massive composite stellar population.

quotes.quote: Smith Jarvis (2007) discovered only the second known Lyman- Blob thought to be ionized by the process of cold accretion, residing at z = 2.83 . In figure blob_contour we show the Sloan-g' band image of the LAB from our survey, overlaid with a contour map of the \ narrow-band data, sensitive to Lyman- emission at the redshift of the LAB. This LAB was found to be extended over at least 95 kpc, with a Lyman- luminosity of L_ Ly = 2.1 0.5 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . The LAB in question is located at 17 ^h 09 ^m 52.3 ^s +59 ^ 13 ^ 21.72 \ (J2000), on the very edge of the Extragalactic component of the Spitzer First Look Survey (Marleau , 2004, Fadda , 2006), and was covered with any S N at all only in the 4.5 m band (IRAC channel 2 - Lacy et al., 2005).

With our new IRAC, MIPs and MAMBO-2 data, as well as our new deep U, B V band observations, we have demonstrated that the counterpart galaxy associated with the newly-discoverd LAB announced in Smith Jarvis (2007) is well-described by a composite stellar population. Using this new multi-wavelength data-set, we find no plausible evidence for the presence either of an obscured active galactic nucleus or of an energetic starburst that would be required to ionize the neutral Hydrogen gas in galaxy-wide super-wind schemes. The most likely source of ionization for this particular highly luminous Lyman- halo is a ``cold accretion'' scenario, which may be able to supply sufficient energy for this profuse emission without invoking either of the other power sources suggested in the literature (see Fardal \ 2001, Dijkstra, , 2006a,b, 2007).

gmw_00004703.name: PKS 2149-306
gmw_00004703.overview: PKS 2149-306 is a high-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar and blazar located at a redshift of 2.345 [arXiv:0902.1789, arXiv:0810.5243]. The galaxy exhibits elevated gamma-ray emission states [arXiv:2401.14872] and a doubling of its hard X-ray flux [arXiv:0810.5243] that can be modeled by changes in its jet bulk Lorentz factor [arXiv:0902.1789, arXiv:1703.08873].

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 2149-306 as a high-redshift FSRQ/blazar, analyzes its optical-to-X-ray variability, and models two SED states through a jet Lorentz-factor change.

quotes.quote: We study the blazar nature of the high-redshift Flat-Spectrum Radio Quasar PKS 2149-306 ( z = 2.345 ) by investigating its long-term behavior. We analyzed all publicly available optical-to-X-ray observations performed by XMM-Newton , Swift , and INTEGRAL . PKS 2149-306 is one of four blazars at z>2 that have been observed in the hard-X-ray regime with both the BAT and ISGRI instruments.

The overall SED of PKS 2149-306 is typical of FSRQs at high redshift, with the Inverse Compton mechanism dominating the spectrum from X- to rays and in accordance with the standard blazar sequence MARASCHI08 . The two different SEDs can be explained in terms of a change only in the jet bulk Lorentz factor, being =15 for the ``high-flux'' state and =10 for the ``low-flux'' state.

sources.summary: PKS 2149-306 shows elevated gamma-ray states, including eight intervals above 1e-6 photon cm-2 s-1 and another active period with flux consistently above 1e-7.

quotes.quote: For example, the time-averaged \ flux of PKS 2149-306 ( z=2.345 ) is (2.01 0.47) 10^ 7 photon cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , but there was a notable period of elevated \ emission between MJD 56288.92 and 56444.75 (Fig. strong_gray panel b). During this interval, the flux above 144.85 MeV exceeded 10^ 6 photon cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 on eight time intervals. The peak flux during this period was (1.68 0.33) 10^ 6 photon cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 observed on MJD 56302.61. Additionally, the source exhibited another active \ emission state between MJD 55554.39 and 55650.67. In this period, the \ flux consistently exceeded 10^ 7 photon cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 , with a maximum of (7.08 1.22) 10^ 7 photon cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 observed on MJD 55613.98.

sources.summary: The paper treats 2149--307 as a z>2 radio-loud blazar detected by Swift/BAT, with hard XRT/BAT spectra and soft-medium X-ray emission.

quotes.quote: We report on \ observations of four z>2 radio-loud quasars (0212+735, 0537--286, 0836+710, and 2149--307), classified as blazars. The sources, well-known emitters at soft-medium X-rays, were detected at >5 with the BAT experiment in 15--150 keV. No flux variability was detected within the XRT and BAT exposures, with the exception of 0836+710 which shows an increase of a factor 4 of the 15--150 keV flux on a timescale of one month. The 0.3--10 keV spectra are well fitted by power law models, with rather hard continua (photon indices _ XRT 1.3-1.5 ); similarly, the 15--150 keV spectra are described by power laws with _ BAT 1.3-1.8 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2149-307 as a high-redshift blazar serendipitously detected by INTEGRAL, with hard X-ray flux roughly doubling relative to Swift/BAT while the spectral slope stayed unchanged.

quotes.quote: PKS 2149-307 (z=2.345) This high-redshift blazar was detected serendipitously in 2004 during an observation of the nearby Seyfert 2 active nuclei NGC 7172 (distance 2.6^ , see Fig. fig:PKS2149 ). The INTEGRAL /IBIS spectrum in the 20-100 keV has the same shape of that detected in 2005 by Swift /BAT during its first nine month of operations , but it is a factor two lower . Therefore, over about one year, the hard X-ray flux of this blazar roughly doubled its value, without changes in slope.

sources.summary: The paper describes PKS 2149--306 as a high-redshift blazar with flat hard X-ray spectra; INTEGRAL, Swift, and XMM-Newton data revealed two Lorentz-factor-modeled states.

quotes.quote: Examples of the good synergy between INTEGRAL and XMM-Newton are the high-redshift blazars PKS 0537-286 ( z = 3.1 ) and PKS 2149--306 ( z = 2.345 ) whose flat hard X-ray spectra made them optimal targets for INTEGRAL .

PKS 2149--306 was observed by INTEGRAL IBIS and Swift BAT at hard X-rays. The comparison, together with XMM-Newton data, puts in evidence two different states, that can be reproduced solely by a change of the bulk Lorentz factor of the jet (Bianchin et al.

gmw_00004704.name: UGC 3193
gmw_00004704.overview: UGC 3193 is a close-to-edge-on barred spiral galaxy located in a galaxy group [arXiv:1705.09762]. It hosts a known maser disk [arXiv:1902.05545] and exhibits two large-scale spiral arms wound clockwise [arXiv:1705.09762].

sources.summary: The paper classifies UGC 3193 as a close-to-edge-on barred-Sab group member with clear spiral arms, a large-scale bar, and possible disk, bulge, and bar structures near its nucleus.

quotes.quote: UGC 3193 The galaxy morphology for UGC 3193 is barred-Sab 1991Vaucouleurs and it is located 61.3 Mpc from Earth 2000Mould , seen close to edge-on. It is a member of a galaxy group. The spiral structure is clear, with two large-scale arms wound clockwise. A large-scale bar is identified in the ellipse profiles at 3'' .

The nuclear region we identify shows a clear maximum in ellipticity profile at 0.8 arcsec ( 220 pc), corresponding to a minimum in position angle. A distinct feature is also visible in the surface brightness ellipse profile. While there appears to be a dust structure at the selected region, it does not straightforwardly point at its morphological type. However, as the ellipse fits contain prominent signatures of the flattened character of that structure, we treat it as a possible disk feature.

sources.summary: The paper detects UGC 3193 in radio continuum, finds matching north-south extended emission at 33 GHz and C-band, and notes that its maser disk orientation and position remain unknown.

quotes.quote: Five out of 18 galaxies observed were detected at 8 or higher levels (Mrk 0001, Mrk 1210, Mrk 1419, NGC 2273 and UGC 3193). While all these sources are known to have maser disks, four of these five sources exhibit a maser disk with known orientation.

UGC 3193 In our 33 GHz data, we see extended emission in the north-south direction, the same as what we observe in the C-band VLBA data. The southern component is not resolved in the Gaussian deconvolution. The maser disk orientation and position is not yet known for UGC 3193.

For all images, phase self-calibration was employed to improve the image quality, using a solution interval of 1 minute. For Mrk 0001 the S/N was improved from 8 to 32, for Mrk 1210 from 36 to 62, for Mrk 1419 from 12 to 18, for NGC 2273 from 56 to 61 and for UGC 3193 from 8 to 36.

gmw_00004705.name: PKS 0208 512
gmw_00004705.overview: PKS 0208 512 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2104.12130] and MeV blazar [arXiv:1002.4030, arXiv:2104.12130] located at a redshift of z = 1.003 [arXiv:1405.7694, arXiv:2104.12130]. The active galaxy exhibits multiwavelength variability [arXiv:1002.4030, arXiv:2008.08137], including gamma-ray flares [arXiv:1405.7694, arXiv:2104.12130] and a reported 2.6-year periodicity [arXiv:2111.06882, arXiv:2403.01520].

sources.summary: The paper reports INTEGRAL and Swift observations of the blazar PKS 0208-512, finding X-ray variability and a marginal 0.5-1 MeV excess that could indicate electron-positron plasma in its jet.

quotes.quote: The active galaxy PKS 0208-512, detected at lower energies by COMPTEL, has been claimed to be a MeV blazar from EGRET. We report on the most recent INTEGRAL observations of the blazar PKS 0208-512, which are supplemented by Swift ToO observations. The high energy X-ray and ray emission of PKS 0208-512 during August - December 2008 has been studied using 682 ks of INTEGRAL guest observer time and 56 ks of Swift /XRT observations.

At X-ray energies (0.2 -- 10 keV) PKS 0208-512 is significantly detected by Swift /XRT, showing a power-law spectrum with a photon index of 1.64. Its X-ray luminosity varied by roughly 30 during one month. At hard X-/soft ray energies PKS 0208-512 shows a marginally significant ( 3.2 ) emission in the 0.5-1 MeV band when combining all INTEGRAL /SPI data. Non-detections at energies below and above this band by INTEGRAL /SPI may indicate intrinsic excess emission. If this possible excess is produced by the blazar, one possible explanation could be that its jet consists of an abundant electron-positron plasma, which may lead to the emission of an annihilation radiation feature.

sources.summary: The paper discusses PKS 0208-512 as a z=1.003 blazar with moderate gamma-ray flares, variable Compton dominance, and modeled flare-location constraints.

quotes.quote: PKS 0208-512 at MJD 55750 PKS 0208-512 ( z = 1.003 , d_ L 6.7 Gpc ) showed several gamma-ray flares of moderate luminosity, which were studied in detail by . What is interesting about these flares is that they show significantly variable Compton dominance parameter. Here we discuss the constraints on the parameters of one of the brightest gamma-ray flares produced by this source, peaking around MJD 55750. Preliminary results for this event were presented in . Following that work, we adopt the following parameter values: L_ 1.7 10^ 47 erg s^ 1 , t_ var,obs 2 d , q = L_ /L_ syn 3.3 , L_X 3.5 10^ 45 erg s^ 1 , 0.7 , L_ d 8 10^ 45 erg s^ 1 , 0.1 , r_ BLR 0.09 pc , and r_ IR 2.2 pc .

While adopted D / 1.4 , here we will use D / = 1 as we do for all other sources. We also adopt a black hole mass of M_ BH 1.6 10^9 M_ 2004ApJ...602..103F . Our constraints for the gamma-ray flare in PKS 0208-512 are shown in Figure fig_pks0208 .

sources.summary: The paper uses PKS 0208 512 as a two-night AGN light-curve example: both tests find first-night variability, but the C-parameter non-variable second-night classification is judged more reliable.

quotes.quote: As for the differences between the F test and the C parameter, we take as an example the case for PKS 0208 512. It was observed along two consecutive nights, 03-04 Nov. 1999, with 40 and 39 points in the DLC, respectively. According to the night logs, both nights had similar atmospheric conditions, with seeing around 2.5 to 3 arcsec and with the presence of some veil and scattered clouds. The values of were close to one: 0.973 and 0.934; and those of _2 were 0.005 and 0.003. Using field stars with published standard magnitudes, it was possible to have the standard magnitude curves for the AGN.

The peak-to-peak amplitudes were m = 0.136 mag (first night) and 0.023 mag (second night), with m_ v =15.857 0.004 and m_ v =15.814 0.004 , respectively, which yielded _1= 0.046 (night 1) and _1=0.006 (night 2). The source resulted variable in the first night according to both C and F tools, and non-variable for the C criterion and variable for the F test during the second night. Taking into account the results of Section sec:resul-gral , we conclude that the reliable classification was that obtained with the C parameter.

sources.summary: The paper presents Fermi/Swift timing and spectral analysis of PKS 0208-512, an FSRQ/MeV blazar at z=1.003, focusing on its 2019-2020 flare with day-scale subflares.

quotes.quote: We present the temporal and spectral study of blazar PKS 0208-512, using recent flaring activity from November 2019 to March 2020, as detected by Fermi-LAT, Swift-XRT/UVOT observatories. The source was in a low ray flux state for a decade and started flaring in November 2019, which continues until March 2020. During the activity state, 2-days binned ray lightcurve shows multiple-peaks indicating sub-flares.

PKS 0208-512 is classified as a flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ), located at redshift z = 1.003 ( 2008ApJS..175...97H ). The Compton Gamma-Ray Observatory ( CGRO ) detected the excess emission at soft gamma-rays, which produced a broad bump shape at MeV energies ( 1995A&A...298L..33B ), therefore the source is also known to be a `MeV blazar'. Later, even brighter excess emission was detected by INTEGRAL/SPI in the 0.5-1 MeV ( 2010A&A...514A..69Z ). The Parkes radio surveys discovered the source ( 1964AuJPh..17..340B ) and was observed regularly at rays ( 1995ApJS..101..259T ) with EGRET on board CGRO . PKS 0208-512 is the ray blazar that has been monitoring regularly by the Fermi Large area telescope (LAT) observatory since August 2008 ( Atwood_2009 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats PKS 0208-512 as a flat spectrum radio quasar with reported 2.6-year ray oscillations, then predicts binary-driven infrared and optical modulation if the periodicity is orbital.

quotes.quote: In this section, as a basis for an illustrative discussion, and to limit our scope, we focus primarily on a specific flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) for which there is a recent claim of quasi-periodic oscillations at a period of 2.6 years in rays: PKS 0208-512 penil+2020 . This object is at a redshift of z 1 0208redshift , which means the source frame periodicity is 1.3 years. If this periodicity corresponds to the orbital period of a putative binary, then it is similar to the fiducial model we studied in this work.

Assuming PKS 0208-512 hosts a near-equal mass, near-circular binary, we predict that the thermal disk emission in infrared and optical (or far-infrared and near-infrared in the observer frame due to cosmological redshift /(1+z) ) should exhibit in-phase ( < 20 days in the observer frame) brightness modulations on near-orbital time scales, with a prominent lump period of 4.5 - 16 times the orbital period. The lump period will be more prominent (relative to the orbital period) in lower-energy disk emission. Given the semi-major axis of a (2-5) 10^ 3 pc implied by the inferred binary mass range and a 1.3-year orbital period, the maximum orbital velocity is on the order of v/ 0.03-0.08 for a circular binary and v/ 0.05-0.13 for an eccentricity of e=0.45 . Since PKS 0208-512 is a blazar (i.e. viewed nearly face-on), the line-of-sight orbital velocities are likely well below the levels in sec:dop . Thus, Doppler brightening on the orbital time scale is very likely irrelevant, so we can predict that a binary would produce spiky orbital periodicity, with the near-infrared light curves (observer frame) having more significant ( 3.3-8.5 ) RMS variability in comparison to far-infrared ( 0.90-2.6 ).

sources.summary: Suzaku spectra of PKS 0208 512 are fit by an absorbed power law, with residuals indicating soft X-ray excess that may become prominent when the source is fainter.

quotes.quote: PKS 0208 512 The time averaged, background subtracted three XIS spectra of PKS 0208 512, when fitted jointly, are well described by a single absorbed power-law model, and the absorption column is consistent with the Galactic value N_ H = 3.08 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 (Dickey Lockman 1990). We obtained the best fit photon index = 1.68 0.02 and the 2-10 keV flux F_ 2-10 keV = (1.37 0.03) 10^ 12 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 with a chi-squared value of 0.91 for 250 dof. Figure fig:QHBspec shows the spectra obtained with the XISs with residuals plotted against the best-fit power-law model with Galactic absorption.

The residuals indicate significant soft excess emission below 1 keV, if N_ H is the same as found in the previous BeppoSAX observation. The variable soft X-ray emission of PKS 0208 512 may indicate that the convex spectrum observed by BeppoSAX reflects an intrinsic IC continuum shape, while the soft excess observed by Suzaku reflects the presence of an additional spectral component which becomes prominent when the source gets fainter (see Tavecchio et al. 2007; Kataoka et al. 2008).

sources.summary: The paper reports a 2.6-year multiwavelength period for PKS 0208 512, optical and X-ray correlations without lag, and cross-correlation periods near 2.7 years.

quotes.quote: Finally, a period of 2.6 yr is reported for PKS 0208 512, which is compatible with P20 (2.7 yr) but inconsistent with P22 (3.8 yr). The local significance of the results typically ranges from 2 to 5 , depending on the method.
PKS 0208 512 has a time lag between the ray and optical bands compatible with 0 days and a local significance of > 4.0 , in agreement with cor_chatterjee_PKS_0208_512 .

Regarding PKS 0208 512, the cross and the autocorrelations show periods compatible of 2.7 yr, which again is compatible with P20 but inconsistent with P22. We also observe similar periodicity in X-rays in the autocorrelation, with a lag 0 days.

gmw_00004706.name: MG1 J021114+1051
gmw_00004706.overview: MG1 J021114+1051 is a BL Lacertae object and low-energy-peaked blazar [arXiv:1105.0572] that features a relativistic jet [arXiv:1105.0572]. The source exhibits high, variable optical polarization [arXiv:1105.0572] and has shown inconsistent periodicity in its emission [arXiv:2403.01520].

sources.summary: The paper presents first detailed optical polarimetry of CGRaBS J0211+1051, identifies it as a featureless-spectrum radio/BL Lac source, and links its high, variable polarization to low-energy-peaked blazar behavior.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of high polarization in the first detailed optical linear polarization measurements on the BL Lac object CGRaBS J0211+1051, which flared in rays on 2011 January 23 as reported by Fermi. The observations were made during 2011 January 30 - February 3 using photo-polarimeter mounted at the 1.2m telescope of Mt Abu InfraRed Observatory(MIRO). The CGRaBS J0211+1051 was detected to have 21.05 0.41 degree of polarization (DP) with steady position angle (PA) at 43 ^ on 2011 January 30.

CGRaBS J0211+1051, also known as MG1 J021114+1051, 1FGL J0211.2+1049, 87GB 020832.6+103726 etc, (RA 02:11:13.2, DEC 10:51:35, J2000) was found to have a featureless optical spectrum and R-band magnitude of 15.42 in optical characterization of bright blazars in the uniform all sky survey, CGRaBS Healey08 . It was identified as radio source and/or BL Lac object in 8.4GHz survey of bright, flat-spectrum radio sources Healey07 and in several other surveys, including 4775 MHz survey Lawrence86 .

The intra-night activity shown by the source appears to be related to turbulence in the relativistic jet. Sudden change in the PA accompanied by a rise in the DP could be indicative of the fresh injection of electrons in the jet. The detection of high and variable degree of polarization categorizes the source as low energy peaked blazar.

sources.summary: The paper finds MG1 J021114+1051 has an inconsistent 1.8-year period, problematic radio-band periodicity, no clear radio-gamma correlation, and optical lags without detected periodicity.

quotes.quote: Similarly, the period of 1.8 yr for MG1 J021114+1051 is inconsistent with the 2.9-year period from P20 and P22.

Furthermore, no clear correlation is found between the radio and ray emission for PG 1246+586 and MG1 J021114+1051. For the latter, a hint of correlation (3.0 of local significance) appears with a time lag of 146.5 31.9 days, contrary to the typical behavior between these bands.

MG1 J021114+1051 has lags compatible with 0 days in the optical band, but no periodicity is detected with the previous methods (see Table tab:periodicity_results ). This result is due to the low sampling of the optical LCs.

gmw_00004707.name: TXS 1902+556
gmw_00004707.overview: TXS 1902+556 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2206.07614] classified as a blazar [arXiv:2403.01520]. It exhibits a periodic signal, with studies identifying periods of 1120 days [arXiv:2206.07614] or approximately 3.6 years [arXiv:2403.01520].

sources.summary: The paper identifies TXS 1902+556 as one of the next most significant periodic candidates, finding a 1120-day period that disagrees with prior lower-significance literature results.

quotes.quote: The next sources with the most significant periodic signal are PKS 2155-304 and TXS 1902+556, with p_ values below 0.005. For TXS 1902+556, a period of 1120 days ( 3.1 years) is identified. This value is not in agreement with previous literature periodic analysis, , where a period of 3.8 years is found in the low significance level ( >2.5 ). On the other hand, for PKS 2155-304, the fitted periodic signal 614 days ( 1.7 years) is compatible with literature. Both sources show also a linear trend and an auto-regressive noise behaviour.

sources.summary: The paper highlights TXS 1902+556 as especially interesting: its optical/radio-gamma cross-correlations show sinusoidal behavior consistent with a 3.6-year periodic signal and earlier results.

quotes.quote: The results of Table tab:periodicity_results also reveal compatible periods with those reported in P20 and P22 in 5 of them: PG 1246+586, S4 1144+40, S4 0814+42, PKS 0301 243, and TXS 1902+556.

Especially interesting is the behavior of TXS 1902+556. The cross-correlations between the optical/radio LCs with the ray emission display a clear sinusoidal behavior expected from a periodic signal see mcquillan2013 , with a period of 3.6 0.2 years and a local significance between 2 3 (see Figure ZDCF_results ), which is compatible with the results of Table tab:periodicity_results . This is also compatible with the results from P22 and supports the hint of periodic behavior for this blazar.

gmw_00004708.name: PG 1246+586
gmw_00004708.overview: PG 1246+586 is a blazar [arXiv:1701.04305, arXiv:2403.01520] classified as a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1701.04305]. The source exhibits a featureless spectrum [arXiv:1701.04305] and displays significantly stronger variability in radio wavelengths than in the optical V and R bands [arXiv:2403.01520].

sources.summary: The paper finds BZB J1248+5820 featureless at high SNR, does not confirm a catalogued SDSS redshift, and derives a modest lower limit.

quotes.quote: BZB J1248+5820 : The source was classified as a BLL by fleming1993 and no redshift was available. The featureless nature of the spectrum is reported in henstock1997, plotkin2008 and shaw2013a . Note that NED report z = 0.847 based on the SDSS DR3 spectrum, although this is not confirmed by SDSS DR13 analysis. scarpa2000 failed to detect the host galaxy from HST images. Our high SNR spectrum is featureless and we can determine a lower limit to the redshift of z > 0.14.

sources.summary: The paper finds PG 1246+586 has periods compatible with earlier gamma-ray work, no clear radio-gamma correlation, and unusually stronger radio variability than optical variability.

quotes.quote: The results of Table tab:periodicity_results also reveal compatible periods with those reported in P20 and P22 in 5 of them: PG 1246+586, S4 1144+40, S4 0814+42, PKS 0301 243, and TXS 1902+556.
Furthermore, no clear correlation is found between the radio and ray emission for PG 1246+586 and MG1 J021114+1051.

PG 1246+586 is the only source of our sample with a significantly higher F_ var in radio, with F_ var ^ radio =0.71 0.02 , compared to F_ var ^ V, R =0.27 0.01 found in the V and R optical bands. This indicates that the emission is more variable in radio than in optical wavelengths for this blazar. However, the coverage and sampling of the radio data set are higher than that in the latter wave bands.

gmw_00004709.name: S4 1144+40
gmw_00004709.overview: S4 1144+40 is a blazar and active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2403.01520]. It exhibits a positive gamma-ray correlation lag of approximately 51.5 days and possesses radio jets [arXiv:2403.01520].

sources.summary: The paper finds S4 1144+40 has periods compatible with earlier work, a positive gamma-ray correlation lag, and optical/radio period differences limited by sparse optical coverage.

quotes.quote: The results of Table tab:periodicity_results also reveal compatible periods with those reported in P20 and P22 in 5 of them: PG 1246+586, S4 1144+40, S4 0814+42, PKS 0301 243, and TXS 1902+556.
We also see a positive correlation for S4 1144+40 with a lag of 51.5 17.3 days.
The low temporal coverage of the V-band LC of S4 1144+40 does not allow us to compare its slope with the one obtained from the radio data set.

gmw_00004710.name: J0905+5759
gmw_00004710.overview: J0905+5759 is a compact starburst galaxy [arXiv:1406.5206, arXiv:1205.2368] with an effective radius of approximately 100 parsecs [arXiv:1406.5206, arXiv:1205.2368]. It hosts a galaxy-wide outflow [arXiv:1205.2368] characterized by a strongly blue-shifted, shell-like absorption profile [arXiv:1406.5206].

sources.summary: The paper models J0905+5759 as a compact starburst/post-starburst galaxy with fast shell-like Mg II absorption whose dusty radiation-pressure shell can roughly match observed velocities and mass.

quotes.quote: In particular, show that the system J0905+5759 has strongly blue-shifted Mg II absorption with a shell-like velocity profile centered at 2470 km s ^ 1 (ranging from 3000 to 2200 km s ^ 1 ), covering the entire galaxy. The effective radius of the galaxy is 100 pc, with stellar mass of 10^ 10.7 M _ , velocity dispersion of 250 km s ^ 1 , and total IR luminosity of 10^ 12.6 L _ (Diamond-Stanic, private communication; see also sell,geach ). Repeating the calculation shown in the left panel of Figure figure:viso , but for =250 km s ^ 1 , R_0=100 pc, and _ tot =2.5 ( L_ bol 1.1 10^ 13 L _ ) we find v_ UV 1900 km s ^ 1 and v_ 2500 km s ^ 1 for f_ sh =0.05 ( M_ sh 1.5 10^8 M _ ).

If interpreted as a shell accelerated by the continuum radiation pressure on dust grains, the shell would have a radial scale of 1.6 kpc, which is consistent with the observed blue continuum. We note that although the velocity and shell mass calculated are roughly consistent with the observational constraints, the required bolometric luminosity of the system is somewhat high compared to the observations.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0905+5759 as the most compact galaxy in its sample, marginally resolved at about 100 pc and showing a high-velocity, galaxy-wide outflow in Keck/HIRES spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: For the most compact galaxy (J0905+5759, r_e=0.013 or 100 pc), we also show the observed one-dimensional surface brightness profile in Figure fig:profile . We compare to the profiles of six stars in the same image, the best-fit de Vaucouleurs model, and a de Vaucouleurs model with r_e=0.04 (the native WFC3/UVIS pixel size, which corresponds to a physical scale of 290 pc). This comparison illustrates that this galaxy, while only marginally resolved with an r_e that is 20 of the image FWHM, is clearly more extended than a point source.
However, the Keck/HIRES spectrum of J0905+5759 ( FWHM 8 ) reveals that the gas covers the entire continuum source near the velocity centroid ( v=-2470 ) indicating a galaxy-wide outflow.

gmw_00004711.name: UGC 5829
gmw_00004711.overview: UGC 5829 is an isolated irregular dwarf galaxy known as the "spider" galaxy due to its distorted, peculiar structure [arXiv:1802.09967, arXiv:2303.05787]. This gas-dominated, star-forming system has a rising rotation curve that requires a dark matter halo at large radii [arXiv:1802.09967, arXiv:2303.05787].

sources.summary: The paper uses UGC 5829 as a well-sampled dwarf whose rising rotation curve requires dark matter at large radii and yields an unphysical maximum-disc mass-to-light ratio.

quotes.quote: UGC 5829 fig:dwarfexamples (left) shows a dwarf galaxy with a well sampled rotation curve: UGC 5829. The observed rotation curve continues to rise out to the last measured point. This is well after the stellar contribution has peaked at 4 kpc. Interestingly, the gas contribution also continues to rise to the last measured point.

When the maximal scaling is applied, matches until 4.5 kpc. At large radii, dark matter is absolutely necessary, albeit one may also scale up the gas component to fully reproduce the rotation curve see 2012MNRAS.425.2299S . Maximizing the stellar disc requires = 4.14 0.08 : this is an eight-fold increase with respect to the pop-synth value of 0.5 M _ / L _ , which seems unphysical. This suggests that UGC 5829 does not have a maximal disc.

sources.summary: UGC5829 is described as the irregular "spider" galaxy, gas-dominated and star-forming, isolated in catalogs, and showing a distorted structure attributed to internal rather than external causes.

quotes.quote: UGC5829=DDO 84=VV 794. An irregular (Im) galaxy with a very peculiar structure that has gotten the name ``spider'' (Fig. 4). More than half the baryon mass of the galaxy belongs to the gaseous component, which evidently triggers numerous sources of star formation in it. UGC 5829 = KIG 434 shows up in the Catalog of Isolated Galaxies (Karachentseva 1973). The nearest relatively massive galaxy NGC 3432 with V_ LG = 578 km/s and D = 9.14 Mpc (Anand et al 2021) is at a projection distance of 3.0 ^ or 470 kpc. UGC 5829 is a distinct example of how a distorted peculiar galactic structure can be created, not by an external perturbation, but by strictly internal properties of a galaxy.

gmw_00004712.name: UGC 10310
gmw_00004712.overview: UGC 10310 is a dwarf galaxy characterized by a relatively flat and featureless rotation curve at large radii [arXiv:1802.09967]. This rotation curve reaches a flat portion at a radius of 5 kpc, where a maximized stellar disc can almost entirely explain the observed rotation [arXiv:1802.09967].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 10310 as a fairly sampled dwarf whose maximized stellar disc nearly explains the rotation curve but requires a mass-to-light ratio above population expectations.

quotes.quote: UGC 10310 fig:dwarfexamples (middle) shows a dwarf galaxy with a fairly sampled rotation curve: UGC 10310. The rotation curve reaches a flat part at 5 kpc. When the stellar disc is maximized, it can almost entirely explains . This is unusual for dwarf galaxies. This is unrelated to the sampling of the rotation curve, but it is due to the fact that is relatively flat and featureless at large radii, so it preserves the salient features in .
The maximum is 2.41 0.06 , which again exceeds the expectations from standard stellar population models.

gmw_00004713.name: UGC 7232
gmw_00004713.overview: UGC 7232 is a dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1504.02084, arXiv:1802.09967] that exhibits recent starburst activity [arXiv:1504.02084]. It features a roughly spherical warm wind [arXiv:1910.04167], extended low-surface-brightness ionized gas [arXiv:1910.04167], and an elevated neutral hydrogen velocity dispersion [arXiv:2303.05104].

sources.summary: The paper uses HST color-magnitude diagrams to show NGC 4190 is a compact dwarf with recent starburst activity, elevated SFR for over 380 Myr, and pronounced outer asymmetries.

quotes.quote: The data for both NGC 4190 and NGC 5204 consist of archival HST imaging obtained using the Wide Field Planetary Camera 2 (WFPC2) with the F606W and F814W filters. Total integration times per filter were 2200 s for NGC 4190 and 600 s for NGC 5204. Table  tab:properties summarizes the observations and basic properties of the systems. Figure  fig:images shows composite color images of both galaxies produced from the Hubble Legacy Archive pipeline. Visible in both galaxies are blue, presumably young, clustered, star-forming regions as well as a more homogeneously distributed redder, presumably older stellar population.

As seen in Figure  fig:sfhs , both NGC 4190 and NGC 5204 have SFRs at recent times that are higher than the b_ recent threshold of 2 and, thus, can be considered starbursts. Moreover, the disc-averaged recent SFRs for both galaxies are elevated for at least 100 Myr, and continue to be higher than the past averages at the present day. The durations of the starbursts, shaded in grey, are >380 130 Myr for NGC 4190 and >100 30 Myr for NGC 5204.

sources.summary: The paper presents UGC 7232 as a poorly sampled dwarf galaxy with only four velocity points, warning that such incomplete data can make it an outlier in maximum-disc analyses.

quotes.quote: UGC 7232 fig:dwarfexamples (middle) shows a dwarf galaxy with a fairly sampled rotation curve: UGC 7232. This object has only four velocity points. While this does not impact fit quality per se, it makes difficult the comparisons with better sampled galaxies. Poorly sampled galaxies like UGC 7232 often appear as outliers in statistical analyses of maximal disc fits ( sub:the_maximal_mass_to_light_ratio ). This does not necessarily mean that they are atypical (though they may be) but it likely indicates that the underlying data is incomplete.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4190 has a roughly spherical warm wind, extended low-surface-brightness ionized gas on both sides of the disk, and prominent southern bubbles.

quotes.quote: NGC 4190 In Figure fig:ngc4190 , NGC 4190 has a warm wind extending roughly spherically around the galaxy with the strongest detection in two approximately orthogonal directions. The ionized gas reaches the edge of both the eastern and western sides of the \ disk; this extended low surface brightness gas does not have a sharp discontinuity in the unsmoothed images (see Figure fig:full_fovb ). There are also a number of prominent \ regions and large \ bubbles in the southern region of the system.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 4190 in the VLA ANGST subsample, finds elevated H I velocity dispersion, and uses it as the low-q end of the stellar-dominated halo range.

quotes.quote: We derive the oblateness parameter q of the dark matter halo of a sample of gas rich, face-on disk galaxies. We have assumed that the halos are triaxial in shape but their axes in the disk plane (a and b) are equal, so that q=/a measures the halo flattening. We have used the velocity dispersion, derived from the stacked emission lines and the disk surface density to determine the disk potential and the halo shape at the R_ 25 and 1.5R_ 25 radii. We have applied our model to 20 nearby galaxies, of which 6 are large disk galaxies with M(stellar) > 10 ^ 10 , 8 have moderate stellar masses and 6 are low surface brightness dwarf galaxies. Our most important result is that gas rich galaxies that have M(gas)/M(baryons) > 0.5 have oblate halos ( q<0.55 ), whereas stellar dominated galaxies have a range of q values from 0.21 0.07 in NGC4190 to 1.27 0.61 in NGC5194.

We also included two galaxies NGC 4190 and UGC 8833 from the VLA ACS Nearby Galaxy Survey Treasury (VLA ANGST) survey ott.etal.2012 . Table 1 shows the sample of 20 galaxies, of which first 8 are relatively large, massive spirals (NGC 0628, NGC 3184, NGC 4736, NGC 5194, NGC 5236 and NGC 6946), and the remaining 12 are smaller spiral, dwarf or irregular galaxies.
